author: Paul Iusztin
subject: Post by Paul Iusztin on X
summary: On 'wiki memory' as an emerging agent-memory pattern (quoting Harrison Chase). Describes combining project-scoped LLM wikis built from personal notes/research with a FastMCP server serving unified memory: instead of RAG over raw documents each time, the agent incrementally builds a small structured Markdown wiki as local working knowledge, with the MCP server handling search, build, sync, and persist. The wiki stays local so you can swap Claude Code for Codex without changing how memory works. Avoids needing GraphRAG across the whole corpus.
content: Memory for agents is still early with little to no standards, but one common pattern is emerging: wiki memory (per Harrison Chase). I built two systems that converged: one builds project-scoped LLM wikis from notes/research/conversations, the other serves unified memory via a FastMCP server. Instead of reasoning over raw documents each time, the agent incrementally builds a small structured Markdown wiki from your unified memory as local working knowledge. FastMCP sits between the two, handling search, build, synchronize changed sources, persist. Doesn't require GraphRAG across the whole corpus; the wiki implements a light KG via file references. Keeping it synchronized (updating only affected pages when sources change) is the hard engineering problem. The wiki stays local so you can switch from Claude Code to Codex without changing how memory works.

author: Paul Hudson
subject: Post by Paul Hudson on X
summary: Shares a reusable 'adversarial unit test sweep' prompt (set Codex/Sol to Ultra reasoning): deliberately target edge cases, malformed inputs, race conditions, boundary values, resource exhaustion, state corruption, and invalid assumptions; make every test earn its place, remove or consolidate weak/redundant tests, fix every failing test, add permanent regression tests for each bug found, and repeat until the suite passes cleanly with strong coverage. Argues AI-driven adversarial test review is one of the easiest high-value practices to adopt.
content: Set Codex/Sol to Ultra reasoning and run: 'Run a comprehensive adversarial unit test sweep that deliberately targets edge cases, malformed inputs, race conditions, boundary values, resource exhaustion, state corruption, and invalid assumptions; ensure every test earns its place by being necessary, non-duplicative, and validating meaningful behavior; remove or consolidate weak or redundant tests; fix every failing test, add permanent regression tests for each bug found, and repeat until the suite passes cleanly with no unexplained failures and strong coverage.' Using AI for unit tests is one of the easiest things to adopt; adversarial review forces each test to earn its place and tests what you think it does.

author: Modal
subject: Post by Modal on X
summary: Modal engineering post on scaling to 1 million concurrent sandboxes, creating all of them in under a minute. They rebuilt the sandbox control plane to remove central coordination: horizontally-scaled scheduling servers run a fast in-memory load-balancing algorithm, each worker is its own source of truth publishing state to a Redis stream, and no datastore sits in the sandbox-creation critical path (two network hops + one CPU op). Contrasts with Kubernetes/etcd O(nodes) and O(pods) bottlenecks; median sandbox start stays under 0.5s at scale.
content: Scaling to 1 million concurrent sandboxes in seconds. Agents run in sandboxes and agents are eating software; RL and background agents need millions of concurrent sandboxes and bursts of hundreds of thousands. Rebuilt core sandbox platform from the ground up: removed all central bottlenecks from the control plane, everything O(sandboxes)/O(nodes) is horizontally scalable. Fleet of scheduling servers handle creation concurrently against in-memory cached data (looks like load balancing); each worker is its own source of truth publishing state to a Redis stream; no datastore in the critical path (two network hops + one CPU op). Created 1M sandboxes in under a minute; median start <0.5s. Contrasts Kubernetes etcd write load O(nodes) and O(n x p) serialized scheduling.

author: Alex Prompter
subject: Post by Alex Prompter on X
summary: 'Harness engineering 101' thread: argues Agent = Model + Harness, where the harness is the software layer around a model (context, tool/permission, verification, memory/state, and safety/sandbox layers). Frames two July 2026 incidents (a ChatGPT Work agent wiping a home directory; xAI's Grok CLI exfiltrating developer repos) as harness failures, and profiles four setups: Anthropic's initializer/planner/coder/fresh-eyes-evaluator, OpenAI's 'repo is the only reality,' Mitchell Hashimoto's one-rule-per-failure, and Continue's shell-aware command parsing. Engagement-farmed framing (save/repost CTAs, product plug) but genuinely substantive.
content: Harness engineering 101. Agent = Model + Harness. The harness is the software infrastructure surrounding an AI model that manages everything except the model's reasoning: tool execution, memory, state persistence, error recovery. Five components: context layer (CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md), tool and permission layer, verification layer (hooks/pre-commit/CI/human review), memory and state layer (files on disk), safety and sandbox layer. Four proven setups: Anthropic (initializer + planner/coder/fresh-eyes evaluator), OpenAI (repo is the only reality, 1M lines / 1500 PRs / 3 engineers / 5 months), Hashimoto (one rule per failure), Continue (parse like the shell, not a censor). Four principles: state lives on disk, failures become rules, verification beats generation, guard structure not strings. Cites July 2026 incidents: a ChatGPT Work agent wiped Matt Shumer's home directory; xAI Grok CLI exfiltrated repos to a GCS bucket.

Ships full code for the orchestrator, experts, and merge step.
content: A Swarm of Agents for Multi-Angle Analysis: Building a Team of Experts from LLMs. Not about speed. About making several agents with different viewpoints argue over one decision and reach a conclusion better than any of them alone, with full code for the orchestrator, experts, and merge. One averaged model view smooths over and finds balance; an important decision must be attacked from different sides. Create several experts, each with a hard-set role and bias (money, technical risk, user), have them analyze one decision independently, then force reconciliation of their conclusions.

author: Aaron Levie
subject: Post by Aaron Levie on X
summary: Aaron Levie's notes from a dinner with enterprise IT leaders on agent adoption: change management is the biggest blocker (processes and data pipelines must be modernized to work with agents), embedding engineers directly into business functions as internal forward-deployed engineers dramatically accelerates successful agent rollouts, and the technical function is becoming more strategically important than ever.
content: Hosted a dinner last night with a group of IT leaders of large enterprises around agent adoption in the enterprise. Quick notes: (1) Change management remains one of the biggest topics for driving workflow transformation — most processes need to be upgraded to modern operating models to work with agents, a mix of technology, data, and human process change, with heavy emphasis on getting structured and unstructured data into a setup that works with agents. (2) IT teams are finding increasing success embedding full engineers into business functions (essentially internal forward-deployed engineers) to implement agents into internal workflows — the technical work is so substantial that having someone technical embedded early accelerates past months of failed experiments. (3) Consensus that the tech function is becoming more important than ever.

author: Jamon Holmgren
subject: Post by Jamon on X
summary: Jamon Holmgren dumps his complete agentic coding setup as a 10+ point checklist: an AGENTS.md that acts as a router to skills/docs/tools; a customized workflow skill (he recommends grabbing Matt Pocock's skills); self-healing, greppable docs with a 7-line summary header; agents that actually run and test the app themselves; e2e tests plus docs on how/what to test; custom precommit linters with --fix that shell out to a cheaper LLM (Composer 2.5 or Sonnet) to actually fix rather than flag; cross-agent review (codex/claude/cursor, never the same model reviewing itself) at research/plan/implementation/wrap-up; handoff worksheets committed with git tags so another agent can finish the job; automatic end-of-session agent feedback docs he periodically ingests to improve workflows; a tools/bin folder of agent-authored scripts (e.g. an agent_review CLI wrapper); and periodic agent sweeps through recent commits. Practical, adoptable patterns for a team running coding agents.
content:
I'm just going to dump my whole agentic setup out here, because I see too many people missing giant chunks of this and it's hurting them.

Here's what I have and recommend:

0. an AGENTS.md that is a router -- it sends the agent to the right skills, docs, tools

1. a standard workflow doc/skill customized to my needs ... (grab Matt Pocock skills if you don't already have something) ... I tag this in most sessions with `@/AGENT_WORKFLOW.md` and it pulls it in.

2. self-healing docs for every system, and agents are instructed to keep them updated ... I tag the ones I know I need, or let the agent find them through AGENTS.md ... I also provide a more detailed summary in the first 7 lines of every doc, so they're easily greppable to find the right thing, and this is documented in AGENTS.md

3. agents always run the app ... the agent should always actually run the app itself, and test its work and fix issues as it goes, especially if running autonomously / asynchronously

4. end-to-end tests and instructions to write more and keep up to date, and docs on how to write tests, what to avoid, and a list of all the tests and what they test in yet another markdown doc ... write and run targeted tests during implementation, improve and commit with work

5. custom linters at precommit hooks looking for any problems you run across, with `--fix` fixing the problems automatically, OR if that's not feasible, it shells out to a cheaper LLM like Composer 2.5 or Sonnet to fix the problems -- NOT just flagging them, but actually resulting in cleaned code

6. cross-agent review at each major point: research, plan, implementation, and wrap-up. I mean codex, claude, cursor, whatever -- but it shouldn't be the same model reviewing the same code. And specific docs for agent review, what to look for, how to approach it.

7. a worksheet: if the agent fails partway through, you should be able to hand this worksheet to another agent and it could finish the job. commit this worksheet with the work so it's all connected and easy to reference later (you will reference these later!!), also have the agent apply git tags that correspond to specific worksheet names so they're easy to find

8. automatic agent feedback to you at the end of the session, added to a doc that is also committed with the work, that you periodically ingest into an interactive session and improve your workflows

9. a tools or bin folder that contains python or bash scripts that the agent has skills to make to make its job easier (for example, I have an `agent_review` bash script that lets the agent kick off agent reviews via CLI without knowing each agent's particular incantations) ... docs on how to make scripts effectively, and instructions to constantly build these out more

10. periodic agent sweeps through recent commits, looking for problems and inconsistencies

subject: Post by How To Prompt on X
summary: How To Prompt (hype framing: "China has killed the vector database industry") flags Tencent's newly open-sourced TencentDB Agent Memory — local long-term memory for AI agents that runs 100% on plain SQLite with no external vector DB or cloud APIs. Claims 61% fewer tokens and PersonaMem accuracy 48%->76%. Uses a layered 'semantic pyramid' (L0 conversation -> L1 atom -> L2 scenario -> L3 persona) stored as inspectable markdown + Mermaid graphs instead of opaque vector compression, with drill-back to raw logs by node_id. ~5.1k GitHub stars.
content: China has killed the entire vector database industry. They open-sourced TencentDB Agent Memory. It gives any AI agent long-term memory that runs 100% locally. No Pinecone. No cloud APIs. 61% fewer tokens; PersonaMem accuracy 48%->76%; zero external API dependencies; runs on plain SQLite. Instead of compressing history into an opaque vector pile, it builds a semantic pyramid: L0 Conversation -> L1 Atom -> L2 Scenario -> L3 Persona. Short-term state encoded as a Mermaid graph in context; verbose tool logs offloaded to disk; drill back via node_id to exact raw log. Every layer is readable markdown. 5.1k stars, 100% open source.

author: Ryan Carson

summary: Ryan Carson (@HelloUntangle) details orchestrating the largest/riskiest engineering program in the company's history with a single Fable parent orchestrator session: 834 files, prod data mutation, DB schema update, 31 PRs, started Friday->completed Monday, zero prod incidents. One parent Devin/Fable session planned the work, spawned ~40 child sessions to execute, enforced regression gates and backup checks between phases, and escalated only owner-level decisions (scope rulings, go/no-go on irreversible steps). Distills reusable program-management patterns for large migrations. In a follow-up he asks Cognition to let child Devin sessions pick their own model/mode independent of the parent.
content:
How to use Fable to orchestrate a huge project with 40 sub-agents. Last week at @HelloUntangle we completed the largest and riskiest engineering program in our history: 834 files, prod data mutation, DB schema update, 31 PRs, started Friday->completed Monday, zero prod incidents, all with one Fable parent orchestrator session. One parent Devin session planned the work, spawned forty child sessions to execute it, enforced regression gates and backup checks between phases, and surfaced only decisions that genuinely required an owner: scope rulings and go/no-go on irreversible steps. Architecture: one orchestrator, many workers.

.@cognition please fix: child sessions created via the Devin MCP can't directly choose the model — they use the same model/mode as the parent. I want to run a Fable 5 parent session and have it spin up child sessions on different models.

author: Akshay
subject: Post by Akshay 🚀 on X
summary: Akshay Pachaar explains building a '1-person AI company' that runs locally, is 100% open-source, has no human employees (all agents), and collaborates in real time via email. His framing: multi-agent orchestration isn't new, but instead of hand-wiring a graph of nodes/edges you should model coordination on the org-chart structure companies have used for a century — lay out an org chart, each agent fills one role with reporting lines, and work flows up/down without manually relaying each message.
content: How to build a 1-person AI company that runs locally, is 100% open-source, has no human employees (all agents), with real-time collaboration via email. Multi-agent orchestration is not new; the interesting question is what structure makes agents collaborate. The common approach wires a graph of nodes and edges. But we've trusted a coordination structure for a hundred years: an organization. People have roles, roles have reporting lines, work moves up and down the chart. Map that onto agents: lay out an org chart, each agent fills one role.

author: kaize
subject: Post by kaize on X
summary: kaize shares a 'Loop engineering' reading list, arguing 2026 agents are less about smarter prompts and more about longer runs — the real questions are whether an agent can recover from a failed step, control spend, and know when to stop. Curated links: Addy Osmani (addyosmani.com/blog/loop-engineering), Firecrawl (firecrawl.dev/blog/loop-engineering), Oracle 'What is the AI agent loop', OpenAI 'Harness engineering', and Martin Fowler 'Harness engineering for coding agent users'.
content:
Loop engineering - the reading list. In 2026 agents stopped being about smarter prompts and started being about longer runs. The real question isn't 'what do I type', it's 'how does my agent keep going for 40 minutes without falling over': Can it recover from a failed step? Can it control spend? Does it know when to stop? It comes back to loop design. Reading list: Addy Osmani - Loop engineering; Firecrawl - Loop engineering; Oracle - What is the AI agent loop; OpenAI - Harness engineering; Martin Fowler - Harness engineering for coding agent users.

Addy Osmani - Loop engineering

OpenAI - Harness engineering

author: 0xSero
subject: Post by 0xSero on X
summary: 0xSero (quote-tweeting Rohan Paul on a Meta paper showing quantized reasoning models often fail because compression makes them doubt a correct answer instead of finishing) reports experimenting with penalizing 'self-doubt' words during generation — claiming ~30% fewer output tokens — plus improving tok/s via CPU offloading.
content:
I found a way to make REAP so much better! Add a penalty to words that lead to self doubt — it's already working, reducing total tokens output by ~30%. Also experimenting with improved tok/s on CPU offloading.

Paper from Meta shows quantized reasoning models often lose because they keep doubting a correct answer instead of finishing. Many reason well enough, but compression makes them hesitate at the wrong time. The problem is post-training quantization.

author: Anatoli Kopadze

Covers what loops are, how they work under the hood, when they're worth it vs a trap, and how to build a basic one in Claude or ChatGPT. Quote-tweets Peter Steinberger: you shouldn't be prompting coding agents, you should be designing loops that prompt your agents. (Includes some self-promotion for his X/Telegram.)
content:
Loops explained: Claude, GPT, Mira and what actually works. Most people who use AI every day still use it the slowest way: type a request, wait, fix it, ask again, all by hand — not because the faster way is complicated, but because nobody showed them. The faster way is a loop, the one thing the best AI engineers care about. You'll learn what loops are, how they work under the hood, when they're worth it and when they're a trap, and how to build a basic one in Claude or ChatGPT.

Your monthly reminder that you shouldn't be prompting coding agents anymore. You should be designing loops that prompt your agents.

author: Elvis
subject: Post by Elvis on X
summary: Elvis makes a meta point about eval-driven skill building that extends beyond coding to any knowledge problem where an eval set can be concretely defined. Example: a newsjack.sh skill that judges newsworthiness — he started from labeled examples (stories that made real news vs ones that didn't, e.g. hitting #1 on Product Hunt isn't news even though LLMs say it is), fed them into an eval set, then used /goal to iterate a skill implementation that lets 3 agents (Opus, Sonnet, Haiku) all score stories correctly — proving 'the intelligence lives in the skill, not the model.' Notes Fable's ability to learn across examples and draw a through-line is well beyond Opus.
content: The meta point applies way beyond coding — you can do this with almost any knowledge problem where an eval set can be concretely defined. Example: I needed a skill in newsjack.sh that determines if something is newsworthy. I started with examples: things that made it into actual news, and things that didn't (hitting #1 on Product Hunt is not news, but LLMs will tell you it is), fed them into an eval set, then /goal toward a skill implementation that would let 3 agents (opus, sonnet, haiku) score all stories correctly — proving the intelligence lives in the skill, not the model. Fable's ability to learn across examples and draw a through-line is way beyond opus.

author: Aaron Levie
subject: Post by Aaron Levie on X
summary: Aaron Levie (Box CEO) argues the battle in AI is shaping up to be a battle for context: agent effectiveness comes down to having the right domain expertise, access to the right context and tools, and being embedded in workflows users can easily review and incorporate. The platforms that capture and leverage the best context within their agents — and pick the right model per task — will be where agents do their best work (coding, legal, support agents at scale). This is why the applied-AI layer is worth far more than being an 'LLM wrapper': the value is in organizing the critical knowledge.
content: The battle in AI is shaping up to be a battle for context. Everything in AI is about making agents as effective as possible, which comes down to whether the agent has the right domain expertise, access to the right context and tools, and is involved in workflow so users can interact, review, and incorporate its work. The platforms that capture and leverage the best context within their agents — and pick the right models for the task — will be where agents do their best work (coding, legal, support agents at scale). This is why the applied AI layer has more value than an LLM wrapper: the ability to organize the critical knowledge.

author: Sprytix
subject: Post by Sprytix on X
summary: Sprytix (clickbait 'Anthropic just leaked an internal engineering document' framing) lays out a six-layer self-improving agent loop: Generate -> Evaluate -> Remember -> Schedule -> Optimize -> Recurse. Generation produces its own solutions (no human brief), Evaluation is a second layer that can say no, Memory retains useful discoveries each cycle, Scheduling decides what happens next, Optimization updates behavior based on what worked, and Recursion means removing any single layer drops performance significantly — shifting the human from operator to designer.
content: ANTHROPIC JUST LEAKED AN INTERNAL ENGINEERING DOCUMENT. The highest-leverage AI systems are no longer prompt-driven, they are loop-driven: Generate -> Evaluate -> Remember -> Schedule -> Optimize -> Recurse. Six layers, one loop, improves itself without a human. Generation produces its own solutions (no human writes the brief); Evaluation measures quality (the thing that can say no); Memory retains useful discoveries each cycle; Scheduling decides what happens next; Optimization updates behavior based on what worked; Recursion — remove any layer and performance drops significantly. The human shifts from operator to designer.

met an anthropic engineer making $1.2M a year. asked how he ships alone at the pace of a full team. he sent me his .claude/. one folder. author: Akshay
subject: Post by Akshay 🚀 on X
summary: Akshay Pachaar summarizes a Hugging Face blog post on 'evolving the harness' instead of training the model: they took a frozen open model scoring 0% on a hard legal-agent benchmark, left its weights untouched, and let an automated loop rewrite only the surrounding harness code (the runtime wrapper that feeds context, runs tool calls, and decides when a run ends). It ended up essentially matching Sonnet 4.6 on the headline metric at ~7x lower cost per task, zero weights changed. The insight: the 0% wasn't measuring legal reasoning — the model reasoned correctly but saved outputs under the wrong filename/folder or not at all — so it was measuring the harness.
content: Don't train the model, evolve the harness. A Hugging Face blog post took a frozen open model scoring 0% on a hard legal agent benchmark, left its weights alone, and let an automated loop rewrite only the code around it. That code layer is the harness — the runtime wrapper that feeds the model context, runs its tool calls, and decides when a run ends. By the end the system essentially matched Sonnet 4.6 on the headline metric at ~7x lower cost per task, zero weights changed. The 0% was never measuring legal reasoning: the model did the analysis correctly then saved it under the wrong name, dropped it in a scratch folder, or never wrote it. It was measuring the harness.

content:
Dhilip Subramanian @sdhilip
My Claude Code setup used to be 3 skills. Now it's 7. Each one earned its spot.
data engineering (@AnthropicAI) → dbt and Airflow skills that slot into data pipeline work.
gstack (@garrytan) → Turns Claude Code into a virtual team that questions what you're building before code starts.

Start small. Add one only when you hit a job your current skills can't do.
Next: compound engineering (@trevin).

author: Andrew Ng
subject: Post by Andrew Ng on X
summary: Andrew Ng's 'loop engineering' letter lays out three nested loops for building 0-to-1 products: the fast agentic coding loop (agent writes/tests/iterates every few minutes), the developer feedback loop (human steers over tens of minutes to hours), and the slow external feedback loop (alpha testers, A/B tests over days). Humans retain a context advantage, so engineers increasingly play a partial product-management role.
content:
Andrew Ng @AndrewYNg
"Loop engineering" letter. Andrew Ng shares his 3 key loops for building 0-to-1 products.
1) Agentic coding loop: AI agent writes code, tests, iterates until bug-free and meets spec. Executes every few minutes; game changer for letting agents work long without human intervention.
2) Developer feedback loop: developer examines product and steers agent to improve. Operates over tens of minutes to hours. Frees devs from being QA so they make higher-level product decisions.
3) External feedback loop: friends feedback, alpha testers, A/B testing in production. Slow (hours to weeks). Data informs developer vision -> spec -> coding agent.
Humans retain a context advantage ("taste") so human-in-the-loop remains needed. Engineers increasingly play partial product-management role; hardest part is shaping product vision. References Boris Cherny (Claude Code) and Peter Steinberger. Source: The Batch.

author: Boris Cherny
subject: Post by Boris Cherny on X
summary: As engineering/product/design/DS roles blur, Cherny proposes five team archetypes (not tied to job function): Prototyper, Builder, Sweeper, Grower, Maintainer. Healthy teams need different mixes by product maturity — pre-PMF wants 1+2+3, growing wants 2+3+4+some 5, strong-PMF wants 3+4+5+some 2.
content: Reflecting on future roles as engineering/product/design/DS melt together. On the Claude Code team, five archetypes: 1. Prototyper (new ideas, most don't ship), 2. Builder (prototype → production), 3. Sweeper (cleans UI, simplifies, unships, optimizes), 4. Grower (iterates to improve PMF), 5. Maintainer (mature system, secure/reliable/fast at scale). Roles aren't tied to job function. Pre-PMF products need 1+2+3; growing need 2+3+4 and some 5; strong-PMF need 3+4+5 and some 2.

author: zostaff
subject: Post by zostaff on X
summary: Long-form "Loop Engineering" article: four autonomous loops that actually pay off because the task repeats, a machine can reject the result, the agent carries it whole, and "done" is objective. Covers the bare while-loop/exit-code/budget mechanics under Claude Code Routines and four worked configs: morning CI test triage (with maker-checker subagent), dependency watchdog, doc synchronizer, and overnight research digest. Theme: the harder the verification, the more you can hand the loop; soft verification keeps a human in the loop.
content: Loop Engineering: Four Loops That Actually Work. Build a loop only when all four hold: task repeats weekly+, something can auto-reject the result, the agent carries it whole, and 'done' is objective. Underneath Routines/`/schedule` is a while-loop + headless agent call + exit-code check, with --max-budget-usd as a brake. Loop 1: morning CI triage with a triage skill + adversarial reviewer subagent on a stronger model (maker-checker). Loop 2: dependency watchdog bumps one package at a time, opens a PR only if green. Loop 3: doc synchronizer fires on PR diffs, proposes doc fixes for human review. Loop 4: overnight research digest, capped at 7 items. The more objective the verification, the more you can hand off; soft verification keeps a human in the circuit. Start with one loop, brakes first, read every diff.

author: Brian Armstrong
subject: Post by Brian Armstrong on X
summary: Coinbase's playbook for keeping AI spend flat while token usage grows: better defaults (experimenting with cheaper open-weight models like GLM 5.2 / Kimi 2.7 via an LLM gateway, since 91% never hit caps), better routing (frontier for planning, cheaper for execution), better caching (LibreChat cache-hit rate 5% → 60%), lean context, and usage visibility. Cut AI spend nearly in half while token usage kept growing.
content: How to keep AI spend flat while token usage grows: not friction and spend alerts, but better defaults, routing, and caching. Better defaults: engineers pick any model but defaults move to cheaper open-weight models (GLM 5.2, Kimi 2.7) via an LLM gateway; 91% never hit caps. Better routing: preprocess prompts and route to the best model (frontier for planning, cheaper for execution); humans shouldn't pick models. Better caching: cache-aware requests took LibreChat from 5% to 60% hit rate. Keep context lean: fresh sessions per task, narrow file scope, disconnect unused tools, don't just compact. Better visibility: usage is visible. Result: AI spend cut nearly in half while token usage keeps growing.

author: Alex Lieberman
subject: Post by Alex Lieberman on X
summary: A "5 levels of work" framework for teaching high agency (credited to @stephsmithio): L1 name a problem; L2 add causes; L3 add possible solutions; L4 add a recommended pick; L5 already fixed it, just keeping you in the loop. Lieberman tells new hires to operate at Level 4 from day one and rise to Level 5 as trust builds.
content: Best illustration of teaching high agency — 5 levels of work. L1: 'There is a problem.' L2: '...and I've found some causes.' L3: '...here are some possible solutions.' L4: '...and here's the one I think we should pick.' L5: 'I identified it, found the cause, researched the fix, and fixed it — just keeping you in the loop.' Every new employee lives at Level 4 from day one and rises to Level 5 as trust builds. (Framework credited to @stephsmithio.)

author: Akshay
subject: Post by Akshay 🚀 on X
summary: Breakdown of "loop engineering" (opening on a Karpathy quote about removing yourself as the bottleneck): a trigger decides what runs, the loop is the maker, a separate checker grades output (a model grading itself just justifies its work), and state lives on disk not context (suggests Zep's Graphiti temporal knowledge graph). Two failure points: set the exit condition before the loop runs, and add observability on the harness since the checker only catches in-run failures (suggests Comet's Opik). Thesis: the model is a commodity; the loop around it is where the engineering lives.
content:
Karpathy: 'Remove yourself as the bottleneck. Put in very few tokens, and a huge amount happens on your behalf.' Loop engineering gets you there. A trigger (message/event/schedule) decides what to run; the loop is the maker that thinks/acts/observes until done or brakes stop it; a separate checker grades output because a model grading itself justifies its own work; state lives on disk (an MD file or knowledge graph), since the model forgets between runs — Zep's Graphiti (github.com/getzep/graphiti) is a clean open-source temporal knowledge graph for that layer. Set the exit condition before the loop runs, and add observability on the harness (prompts/tools/checks drift in production) — Comet's Opik (github.com/comet-ml/opik) turns a failing trace into a regression test. The model is a commodity; the loop around it is where the real engineering lives.

Zep Graphiti repo

Comet Opik repo

author: Avi Chawla
subject: Post by Avi Chawla on X
summary: Avi Chawla explains why BM25, a 30-year-old keyword ranking algorithm with no training or embeddings, still powers Elasticsearch/OpenSearch and excels at exact-match retrieval where embeddings struggle, making hybrid (BM25 + vector) search the default in top RAG systems. He closes on the UX problem of highlighting which span actually drove a semantic match, pointing to his co-founder's article.
content:
Vector search is not always the answer. BM25 (word rarity via IDF, saturating term frequency, length normalization) still powers most production search and nails exact keyword matches embeddings miss, which is why hybrid search is the RAG default. Highlighting why a semantic result matched is hard but non-negotiable in healthcare/legal/finance.

Your RAG System Has a Hidden UX Problem: retrieval is semantic but showing the user why a passage matched breaks down.

author: Codez
subject: Post by Codez on X
summary: A long-form Loop engineering roadmap arguing the leverage point in agentic coding has moved from writing prompts to designing self-running loops. Covers the 4-condition test for when a loop is worth building (task repeats, automated verification, token budget, senior-engineer tooling), the five building blocks (automations like /loop and /goal, git worktrees, skills, MCP connectors, sub-agents), the maker/checker split, the Ralph Wiggum quiet-failure mode, comprehension debt and cognitive surrender, and the security tax of unattended loops. Cites Anthropic engineering docs and Addy Osmani.
content: Loop engineering: the 14-step roadmap from prompter to loop designer. The leverage point moved from typing prompts to designing systems that prompt the agent: an automation that finds work, hands it to the agent, checks the result via an objective gate, records state, and decides the next move. Three tiers: run the 4-condition test, learn the five building blocks (automations, worktrees, skills, connectors, sub-agents), then build the smallest loop that works. Covers maker/checker split, the Ralph Wiggum quiet-failure mode, comprehension debt, and the security tax of unattended loops.

another banger from Sydney! i think this whole hierarchy of loops is still super early but some primitives we know work. ex: verification as a primitive is so ridiculously important for non-slop semi-long-horizon work, it's worth spending days to weeks making sure the distribution of outcomes you want from your agent are verifiable in practice by your system

summary: Explains 'loop engineering' (framed with a Karpathy quote about removing yourself as the bottleneck and maximizing leverage): move the operator's two manual jobs — deciding what the agent runs next and checking its output — into the system itself. A schedule decides what to run, a maker loop produces the work, a separate checker agent grades the output, and a file on disk holds the shared state both read; the loop runs until done, max iterations, or budget is exhausted.
content: Avi Chawla @_avichawla: Karpathy - remove yourself as the bottleneck, maximize leverage, put in few tokens and a huge amount happens on your behalf. Loop engineering moves the operator's two manual steps (deciding what runs next, checking output) into the system: a schedule decides what to run, the loop is the maker, a separate checker agent grades output, a file on disk holds shared state. Runs until done, max iterations, or budget exhausted.

summary: Alibaba open-sourced 'open-code-review' (Apache-2.0), the internal AI code reviewer that's run on their codebase for ~2 years — 20,000+ engineers, 1M+ reviews. Claims ~1/5 the token cost of a generic agent + skills and precise line-level comments that don't drift on large changesets. Repo name: open-code-review.
content: Alibaba open-sourced its internal AI code reviewer (open-code-review), running their codebase for 2 years; battle-tested by 20,000+ engineers, 1M+ reviews, Apache-2.0, one command to install. Not yet another LLM wrapper. Generic-agent pain: skips files on big changesets, line numbers drift, quality changes with prompt tweaks. Alibaba's fix: don't let the LLM do everything. Deterministic engineering handles file selection, smart bundling, rule matching, comment positioning; the agent handles context reading, codebase search, deep reasoning. Result: ~1/5 token cost, precise line-level comments that don't drift, stable on massive changesets.

author: Paweł Huryn
subject: Post by Paweł Huryn on X
summary: Lists six patterns for Anthropic-style dynamic agent workflows/loops: classify-and-act (one agent decides type, code routes), fan-out-and-synthesize (per-piece agents merged in code), adversarial verification (separate rubric-checker), generate-and-filter (many candidates → dedupe → survivors), tournament (judges compare different attempts), and loop-until-done (spawn until a stop condition). Each with a concrete example. Links his 'Claude Dynamic Workflows' guide.
content: Six patterns for building dynamic workflows and loops identified by Anthropic: 1. Classify-and-act: one agent decides the type, the script routes it. Example: bug vs feature vs noise. 2. Fan-out-and-synthesize: one agent per piece, merged in code. Examples: market research, competitor teardown. 3. Adversarial verification: a separate agent checks the output against a rubric. Example: fact-checking a PRD against the sources. 4. Generate-and-filter: many candidates, deduped, the survivors kept. Examples: naming, positioning, ideation. 5. Tournament (compare): agents attempt the task different ways, judges compare until one wins. Example: product strategy. 6. Loop-until-done: spawn until a stop condition. Example: implement, document, and test a feature in one shot. QT: Claude Dynamic Workflows (not only) for PMs: The Ultimate Guide.

summary: Long personal write-up: BOOTOSHI claims the agent-orchestrating-subagents pattern (token-maxxing across parallel work) was right for opus-4.5/gpt-5 but is no longer optimal with the newer generation (gpt-5.5, opus-4.8). Argues the extended context window + intelligence means these models are now more capable as a single 'MEGA THREAD' with a single operator. Counter-trend to the multi-agent enthusiasm of early 2026.
content: i fucked up my sleeping schedule because of my new ai workflow but it's SOO worth it. i feel i have leveled up my engineering productivity to new heights again! (LONG, detailed write up on it below) i've finally found the BEST workflow i've ever used for coding after a lot of trial and error with 'productivity theatre' for ex. having agents orchestrate subagents in attempts to token maxx and try to capture as much work as i can in one shot. while that DID work, and it WAS good (and quite necessary) with older models (opus 4.5, and gpt 5, lol) it is no longer good with the new generation of models (gpt 5.5 and opus 4.8). while the numbers of these models seem like small increments they are COMPLETELY different in capabilities. because of their extended context window and increased intelligence, they are actually more capable BY THEMSELVES in one single MEGA THREAD.

author: Peter Yang
subject: Post by Peter Yang on X
summary: Five takeaways from a conversation with @kunchenguid (ex-Meta L8 engineer) on agentic engineering: (1) plan and validate, don't code yourself — you're the always-on team's manager; (2) plan quality determines how long agents run autonomously — a detailed spec can run for hours vs minutes for a one-liner; (3) use visual plans, not walls of markdown — Lavish (github.com/kunchenguid/lavish) generates visual HTML plans; (4 and 5 truncated in scrape — likely about validation rubrics and feedback loops).
content:
My 5 biggest takeaways from @kunchenguid, ex-Meta L8 engineer, on how he set up his agentic engineering system: 1. Spend most of your time planning and validating, not coding. Kun sees himself as the manager of an always-on engineering team. His job is to create plans, validate work, and improve the overall system. The coding phase is handled almost entirely by agents. 2. The quality of your plans determines how long agents can work on their own. Memes about loops aside, an one-line prompt might keep an agent working for a few minutes but a detailed plan can keep it going for hours. If you want agents to run longer, invest more upfront in the spec, goals, and validation criteria. 3. Use visual plans, not walls of markdown. Kun built Lavish, an open-source tool to make visual HTML plans, that anyone can use for free: https://github.com/kunchenguid/lavish

Lavish on GitHub

author: Ahmad
subject: Post by Ahmad on X
summary: Self-study curriculum for LLM serving engines and GPU kernel programming: start with vLLM / SGLang / TensorRT-LLM / FlashInfer (PagedAttention, continuous batching, prefix caching), then dive into Triton, CUTLASS/CuTe, FlashAttention papers, PagedAttention paper, MoE docs, Nsight profiling. Followed by a hands-on project sequence (Triton RMSNorm, fused SiLU×gate, etc.). For people actually wanting to learn the inference stack.
content: How to go about learning all of this? 1st: Start with the serving engine view - vLLM: PagedAttention, continuous batching, prefix caching, CUDA graphs - SGLang: RadixAttention/prefix reuse, speculative decoding, MoE, structured/agent workloads - TensorRT-LLM: NVIDIA peak stack, FP8/FP4, Wide-EP, disaggregated serving - FlashInfer: reusable kernel/operator library for attention/GEMM/MoE/sampling. 2nd: Go down the stack - Triton tutorials → custom fused kernels - CUTLASS/CuTe → Tensor Core GEMM and Blackwell/Hopper details - FlashAttention papers → attention algorithm/kernel co-design - PagedAttention paper → KV-cache memory management - MoE docs → routing + grouped GEMM + all-to-all - Nsight profiling → stop guessing. 3rd: Do this mini-project sequence: 1. Implement RMSNorm in Triton; compare to PyTorch 2. Implement fused SiLU × gate.

author: Rahul
subject: Post by Rahul on X
summary: Long-form X article framing 'Harness Engineering' as the most important AI discipline of 2026 — OpenAI shipped 1M lines of production code in Feb 2026 using agents wrapped in a reliable system (the 'harness'); Anthropic published 3 papers on it; ThoughtWorks formalized a framework; Philipp Schmid called it the most important discipline of 2026. content: Harness Engineering: What Every AI Engineer Needs to Know in 2026. In February 2026, a small OpenAI team shipped 1 million lines of production code. They didn't write a single line by hand. The AI agents wrote it. The humans designed the system that made the agents reliable. That system has a name now. Harness Engineering. Within weeks, Anthropic published 3 papers on it. ThoughtWorks formalized a framework. Philipp Schmid at Hugging Face called it 'the most important discipline of 2026.' A new engineering discipline materialized in 90 days. And almost nobody outside AI infrastructure teams understands it yet. This article explains everything. PART 1: WHAT A HARNESS ACTUALLY IS — The concept that changes how you think about AI.

author: Viv
subject: Post by Viv on X
summary: A default recipe for optimizing Agent = Model + Harness, 'training' both: (1) build v1 on a sensible base harness with task-specific prompting/tools, (2) harness-engineer against prod-like eval tasks (often enough on its own), (3) SFT on mined traces or synthetic data (good for distilling a cheaper model), (4) RL if you can design environments/rewards to push past SFT copying, (5) light harness engineering again on the trained model. Argues harness engineering will be the dominant optimization lever and most companies are still at steps 1-2; links the 'Anatomy of an Agent Harness' article.
content:
Viv @Vtrivedy10. Default recipe to optimize Agent = Model + Harness; you should 'train' both. 1) Build v1 with sensible base harness + task-specific prompting/tools. 2) Harness engineering using eval tasks matching prod (often enough; collect traces, mine patterns, tweak). 3) SFT from trace data or synthetic (good for distillation to cheaper model). 4) RL if you have bandwidth to design environments/rewards - push past SFT 'copying'. 5) Light harness engineering again with trained model. Loop will be productized as general recipe. Still earliest innings; harness engineering will be dominant way to optimize agents. QUOTE ARTICLE 'The Anatomy of an Agent Harness' - Agent = Model + Harness; harness engineering builds systems around models to turn them into work engines. 57.3K views.

Article: 'The Anatomy of an Agent Harness' — Agent = Model + Harness; harness engineering turns intelligence into a work engine.

author: Thariq
subject: Post by Thariq on X
summary: Full Anthropic article on dynamic workflows in Claude Code: Claude writes its own JavaScript harness to spawn/coordinate subagents (own models, own worktrees, resumable), countering agentic laziness, self-preferential bias and goal drift. Covers patterns (fan-out-and-synthesize, adversarial verification, tournament, loop-until-done), many use cases (migrations, deep research, triage, root-cause, evals, model routing), the 'ultracode' trigger, token budgets, and when NOT to use it.
content:
Thariq @trq212. FULL X ARTICLE 'A harness for every task: dynamic workflows in Claude Code' (also on Claude Blog). Claude Code can now write its own harness on the fly, custom-built per task, and you can share/reuse workflows. Dynamic workflows execute a JavaScript file with special functions to spawn/coordinate subagents; can choose per-agent models and whether subagents run in their own worktree; resumable if interrupted. Motivation: single-context harness suffers agentic laziness, self-preferential bias, and goal drift on long/parallel/adversarial tasks; workflows orchestrate separate Claudes with isolated goals. Dynamic (Opus 4.8 writes custom harness) vs static (Agent SDK / claude -p, more generic). Patterns: classify-and-act, fan-out-and-synthesize, adversarial verification, generate-and-filter, tournament (pairwise judging), loop-until-done. Use cases: migrations/refactors (Bun rewritten Zig->Rust), deep research (/deep-research skill fans out searches + verifies + cites), deep verification, sorting via tournament, memory/rule adherence (mine sessions -> CLAUDE.md rules, one verifier per rule), root-cause investigation (disjoint-evidence hypotheses + verifiers/refuters), triage at scale (classify/dedupe/act, quarantine untrusted content, pair with /loop), exploration/taste, evals, model/intelligence routing (classifier routes Sonnet vs Opus). Trigger word 'ultracode'. Combine with /goal and /loop; set token budgets ('use 10k tokens'); save with 's' to ~/.claude/workflows or distribute via a skill. When NOT to use: uses many more tokens; most regular coding tasks don't need it. Authors: Thariq Shihipar & Sid Bidasaria, Anthropic. 3.2M views.

A harness for every task: dynamic workflows in Claude Code (full article)

author: Rohit Ghumare
subject: Post by Rohit Ghumare on X
summary: Summarizes Mike Piccolo's argument that a harness isn't one thing but ~15 separate concerns (turn state machines, provider routing, credential vaults, policy engines, approval gates, budget trackers, context compaction, session trees, tracing) bundled by frameworks out of necessity. When each layer is a worker on a shared bus with a typed contract, 'build your own harness' means swapping a worker, not forking a framework.
content:
Rohit Ghumare @ghumare64. 'Almost everyone is building agent harness systems the wrong way.' Default: adopt LangChain/LangGraph/OpenAI Agents SDK and accept loop+tools+memory+orchestration+policy+credentials+budget as one decision. Mike Piccolo's argument: a harness isn't one thing - it's ~15 separate concerns bundled (turn state machines, provider routing, credential vaults, policy engines, approval gates, budget trackers, hook fanout, context compaction, session trees, OpenTelemetry tracing) because the ecosystem gave no way to compose them. When every layer is a worker on a shared bus with a typed function contract, 'build your own harness' means swap a worker (custom model catalogue, secrets manager, Slack approvals via approval::resolve) rather than fork a framework. author: Paul Iusztin
subject: Post by Paul Iusztin on X
summary: Breakdown of Neo4j's graph-native agent memory: three connected layers in one graph — short-term (ordered message chain), long-term (entity/relationship knowledge graph with embeddings, traversed relationally), and reasoning memory (per-run trees of thoughts/tool-calls/decision paths). Typed edges (:MENTIONS/:INITIATED_BY/:TOUCHED) make provenance a one-hop query; 'the ontology is the real product.'
content:
Paul Iusztin @pauliusztin_. Study of Neo4j's agent memory system: memory separated into 3 connected layers in the same graph (vs typical conversation history + vector search). 1/ Short-term: active conversation state, linear ordered message chain. 2/ Long-term: persistent knowledge graph of entities/orgs/locations/preferences/facts/relationships as nodes with embeddings, so the agent traverses connected knowledge (relational, not just semantic). 3/ Reasoning memory: stores reasoning traces (thoughts, tool calls, decision paths, intermediate steps) as a tree per run, enabling later analysis of what reasoning/tools/strategies worked - like RL but optimized at the DB layer, not model weights. All three stitched via typed edges (:MENTIONS, :INITIATED_BY, :TOUCHED) making provenance a one-hop query. Takeaway: 'the ontology is the real product'; graph-native memory turns retrieval into traversing relationships/reasoning paths. Deep dive in Decoding AI Magazine (Substack). 2,115 views. agent-design, research.

Understanding Neo4j's graph agent memory (deep dive)

author: Avi Chawla
subject: Post by Avi Chawla on X
summary: Clear explainer of RAG vs Graph RAG vs Agentic RAG as solving different query types: standard RAG for single-hop factual lookups, Graph RAG (LLM-extracted entities/relationships + traversal) for multi-hop connections, Agentic RAG (an agent choosing tools/sources at query time) for dynamic multi-source tasks — plus binary quantization for 32x more memory-efficient vector search.
content:
Avi Chawla @_avichawla. RAG vs Graph RAG vs Agentic RAG explained. Standard RAG: embed docs, retrieve similar chunks - good for direct factual lookups, breaks when a query must connect facts across docs. Graph RAG: LLM extracts entities/relationships at indexing, traversal at retrieval enables multi-hop (worked example: checkout service -> payments API -> cluster-3 -> Friday maintenance; vector search misses the connecting middle fact, graph traversal finds the full path). Agentic RAG: an LLM agent decides at query time which tools/sources to invoke and in what order. Not levels to graduate through - they solve different query types (single-hop=RAG, multi-hop=Graph RAG, dynamic multi-source+tools=Agentic RAG). Efficiency: vector search can be 32x more memory efficient via binary quantization. 181.6K views. research, agent-design, dev-practices.

Article: 'How to make RAG 32x memory efficient (explained with code).'

author: Parag pawar
subject: Post by Parag pawar on X (have a look)
summary: Argues CLAUDE.md is a control layer, not a README: use scope hierarchy (global → project → folder, nearest wins) and a WHAT/WHY/HOW framing, favor specificity ('TypeScript strict mode, Zod validation' over 'production-ready code'), start with /init, keep it under ~500 lines, use hooks, and treat it as living infrastructure.
notes: Jeremy flagged with '(have a look)' — relates directly to project CLAUDE.md practices.
content: Parag pawar @promptparag (was @dharmikpawar13). CLAUDE.md is not a README - it's the control layer for your AI agent; inconsistent/buggy outputs are usually an instruction-architecture problem, not the model. Scope hierarchy: global (coding standards) -> project (stack, workflows, commands) -> folder (local overrides); nearest scope wins. Framework: WHAT (project context) / WHY (principles & constraints) / HOW (execution rules & commands). Specificity separates reliable from chaotic agents (e.g. 'use TypeScript strict mode, Zod validation, async error boundaries' beats 'write production-ready code'; 'Vitest unit tests 80%+ coverage' beats 'add tests'). Rules: start with /init, keep CLAUDE.md <500 lines, use hooks for repeatable behaviors, treat it as living infrastructure, reference configs instead of duplicating logic. 2,080 views. claude-code, prompting, dev-practices.

author: Kevin Simback
subject: Post by Kevin Simback 🍷 on X
summary: A definitive guide to memory in the Hermes Agent, structured as a 3-layer stack: Layer 1 native (two always-injected markdown files MEMORY.md/USER.md plus a searchable SQLite session DB; the 80% consolidation 'rule' is a prompt instruction, not code), Layer 2 the pluggable MemoryProvider slot (8 official providers — Honcho, Mem0, Hindsight, Holographic, OpenViking, RetainDB, ByteRover, Supermemory — one at a time, each a different architectural bet), and Layer 3 community plug-ins (GBrain, Mnemosyne, etc.). Closes with how to pick and warning signs a memory layer is too heavy.
content:
Kevin Simback @KSimback. FULL X ARTICLE 'The Hermes Agent Memory Guidebook' - definitive guide to memory systems for Hermes Agent (Nous Research). Memory turns a stateless chatbot into something that compounds; every serious harness (OpenClaw, Claude Code, Codex, Hermes) has memory primitives, and a product category emerged (Mem0 raised $24M, Letta $10M, Zep, Cipher, Supermemory, Hindsight). Hermes treats memory as first-class pluggable infra in a 3-layer stack. Layer 1 (native, out of box): two markdown files in ~/.hermes/ (MEMORY.md ~2,200 chars general notebook; USER.md ~1,375 chars about you) pasted whole into every prompt (sticky notes, not filing cabinet; agent edits via memory tool add/replace/remove); plus a SQLite session DB (~/.hermes/hermes_state.db) archiving every message/response/tool-call/reasoning-step + token/cost, 90-day retention, searched on demand via FTS5 (word + trigram). Note: no automatic consolidation - the 80% rule is a prompt instruction; the 'Autonomous Curator' (v0.12) only manages the skills library, not memory files. Layer 2 (pluggable MemoryProvider, one at a time via 'hermes memory setup'): 8 official providers - Honcho (models how you think, dialectic user modeling), Mem0 (30-sec setup, broadest ecosystem, AWS Strands, v3 94.8 LongMemEval), Hindsight/Vectorize (benchmark king, 4 networks + 4 parallel retrieval strategies, reflect tool), Holographic (zero-dependency local, HRR compositional, sub-ms), OpenViking (tiered filesystem context DB), RetainDB (cheapest cloud-only, Memory Router), ByteRover (memory as git repo, markdown, 5-tier retrieval, sub-100ms), Supermemory (latency+scale leader, sub-300ms at 100B tokens/mo, context fencing). Layer 3 (community): GBrain (by garrytan, MIT, ~18k stars, skillpack+MCP ~30 tools, 8-layer knowledge engine, epistemology layer, self-wiring graph, overnight dream cycles, Obsidian/Logseq/Notion/Roam migration - author's personal pick), Mnemosyne (fully local <2ms, tiered working/episodic/scratchpad, time-aware 'what did I believe last Tuesday'), Ladybug (local + importance ranking), yantrikdb (explainable 'why retrieved'), hermes-agentmemory (real deletion + audit), PLUR (cross-agent memory bridge via shared .plur/), FlowState-QMD (predictive prefetch). How to pick: match provider to need; warning signs you went too heavy (slower responses, rising API bill, self-contradiction from unconsolidated memory, context exhaustion, no measurable improvement -> pull it). hermesatlas.com. 83.9K views. agent-design, research, skills-mcp.

Hermes Atlas — ecosystem/memory map

author: aditya

summary: A worked answer to a Google L5 interview question — 'design a RAG pipeline for 10M docs with zero hallucination': ingest/normalize, hybrid retrieval (BM25 + embeddings), ANN + reranking, source-confidence scoring, constrained generation, citation-backed responses, hallucination fallback, continuous evals, caching, and observability. At scale, retrieval quality matters more than the frontier model.
content: aditya @adxtyahq. System-design breakdown of a Google L5 interview question: 'design a RAG pipeline for 10M docs with zero hallucination.' 1) ingest+normalize (dedupe, standardize, metadata, versioning), 2) hybrid retrieval (BM25 + embeddings), 3) ANN retrieval + reranking, 4) source confidence scoring (freshness/trust/overlap/consistency), 5) constrained generation (answer only from retrieved context), 6) citation-backed responses, 7) hallucination fallback ('insufficient evidence'), 8) continuous evals (adversarial queries, recall benchmarks, hallucination tests), 9) caching + memory layer, 10) observability (trace retrieval paths, rankings, token attribution). At 10M docs retrieval quality matters more than the frontier model. 197.2K views. research, dev-practices, agent-design.

author: James Cogan
subject: Post by James Cogan on X
summary: A thoughtful essay, 'Machine Time,' arguing AI compresses the unit of meaningful time — shrinking the buffer between noticing a change and having to respond. AI compresses the front end of cognitive work, so the bottleneck moves to human review, judgment, and taste; the danger isn't speed but speed without scaffolding, and institutions increasingly answer with machine-speed coordination rather than restoring the human clock.
content: James Cogan @cogan. X ARTICLE 'Machine Time: Why AI Is Reorganizing the World Around a Clock That Isn't Ours.' Thesis: AI isn't just faster - it compresses the unit of meaningful time, shrinking the buffer between noticing a change and having to respond. Like prior compressions (printing press, telegraph, railroads/standard time, assembly line, containerization, electronic trading, email), efficiency becomes culture: normal delay becomes negligence. AI compresses the front end of cognitive work (drafting/search/codegen), so the bottleneck moves to the human back end - review, judgment, integration, taste, 'absorption.' 'Taste is the bottleneck'; the new creative unit is the high-speed generate/reject/select loop (Higgsfield film example: 26,000 attempts unseen). Danger isn't speed but speed without scaffolding (aviation checklists, surgery time-outs, SRE runbooks). Institutions increasingly answer with machine-speed coordination (agent-to-agent, autonomous payments, async coding agents) rather than restoring the human clock. Apprenticeship problem: AI erodes the junior tasks where judgment forms while increasing senior responsibility. Not speed vs slowness - compression that frees judgment vs compression that overwhelms it. 'Displacement... a world beginning to run on a clock that was not built for us.' 8,286 views. Thoughtful management/industry essay. management, industry, research.

author: Lotte
subject: Post by Lotte on X
summary: 'Evals, explained' (Langfuse Academy): offline eval sits between running an experiment and shipping. Three methods — manual review (build intuition + ground-truth labels), code-based (deterministic checks: schema, keywords, length, SQL executes), and LLM-as-a-judge (language-understanding qualities, needs calibration against human labels). Prefer binary pass/fail over 1-5 scales; one evaluator per quality; start manual then automate repeatable checks.
content: Lotte @lotte_verheyden. X ARTICLE 'Evals, explained' (Langfuse Academy, part of the AI Engineering Loop series). Offline eval sits between running an experiment and shipping; you start with manual review to build intuition and produce ground-truth labels, then automate. Three methods: manual (build intuition, human labels), code-based (deterministic: valid JSON/schema, keywords, length, SQL executes - fast/cheap but can't assess meaning), and LLM-as-a-judge (for language understanding: relevance, tone, summary quality - imperfect, needs calibration against human labels, can share blind spots with your app's LLM). Reference-based vs reference-free (latter works on unseen production data). Practical: prefer binary pass/fail over 1-5 scales; each quality gets its own evaluator; start with manual review then automate only repeatable checks; move some reference-free evaluators into production. 40.4K views. dev-practices, research, agent-design.

author: Akshay
subject: Post by Akshay 🚀 on X
summary: RAG vs CAG explained: Cache-Augmented Generation keeps static, high-value knowledge in the model's KV memory instead of hitting the vector DB every query. Combine them — cache 'cold' static data (policies/docs), retrieve 'hot' dynamic data — for faster, cheaper inference. OpenAI and Anthropic already support prompt caching.
content:
Akshay @akshay_pachaar. RAG vs CAG explained. RAG's problem: every query hits the vector DB even for static info. Cache-Augmented Generation (CAG) has the model 'remember' static info in its KV memory. Combine RAG+CAG: static data (policies, docs) cached once in KV memory; dynamic data (recent updates) fetched via retrieval - faster inference, lower cost, less redundancy. Be selective: only cache static high-value knowledge (else hit context limits); separate 'cold' (cacheable) from 'hot' (retrievable). OpenAI and Anthropic already support prompt caching. QUOTE ARTICLE: 'Prompt caching, clearly explained - how Claude achieves 92% cache hit-rate.' 280.2K views. research, dev-practices, claude-code.

author: Amar Singh
subject: Post by Amar Singh on X
summary: 'Agent Performance: Model-Bound vs Harness-Bound' — counterintuitively, smarter models make harnesses matter MORE ('the smarter the model, the more expensive it is to waste its intelligence'). Model-bound tasks (hard math/proofs) hinge on raw capability; harness-bound tasks (e.g., Terminal-Bench, ~10 pts spread for Opus 4.7 across harnesses) hinge on prompting, tools, context management. As traces balloon and costs rise, the harness becomes the 'scheduler for intelligence'; author's open-source HALO optimizes harnesses from traces.
content:
Amar Singh @AmarSVS. X ARTICLE 'Agent Performance: Model-Bound versus Harness-Bound.' Counterintuitively, as models get smarter, harnesses matter MORE - 'the smarter the models, the more expensive it is to waste their intelligence.' Model-bound task = bottleneck is raw model capability (e.g., hard math/proofs), marginal returns from prompting/tools. Harness-bound task = same model performs drastically differently by prompting, tools, context management, loop design (Terminal-Bench: ~10 pts accuracy difference for Opus 4.7 across harnesses alone). Historical shift: early LLMs were model-bound (bad at instructions); as they improved, harness engineering became valuable - question shifted from 'can the model reason?' to 'did the system expose the right info/tools/context/budget at the right time?'. Context: real agent traces reach hundreds of thousands of tokens, so context management grows more important. Cost pressure -> subagent delegation to smaller/specialized models; the harness becomes the 'scheduler for intelligence' (which models act, get what context, when to escalate/parallelize/stop). Future harness libraries will be 'operating systems for cognition.' Author's tool HALO (open source, context-labs/HALO) optimizes harnesses from traces (gains on AppWorld, Terminal-Bench, Finance-Agent, tau3-Bench). 12.6K views. agent-design, research, dev-practices.

author: Vasilije
subject: Post by Vasilije on X
summary: 'Memory isn't a plugin. Skills aren't a plugin. They're the same harness' — both are a world model (everything the agent uses to predict its next action). Memory observes the world; skills (SKILL.md procedures) codify it into rules and degrade silently without an Observe→Inspect→Amend→Evaluate loop. Cognee stores skills and memory in one graph so a skill is a traceable, evolving memory node.
content: Vasilije @tricalt. X ARTICLE: 'Memory isn't a plugin. Skills aren't a plugin. They're the same harness.' Memory APIs aren't a viable product category and skill systems are just markdown; both are the same harness = a world model (everything the agent is aware of and uses to predict the next action: codebase layout, tool schemas, filesystem, last turns, user preferences). Skills are the part that records what to do (SKILL.md = procedure-level claim); memory observes the world, skills codify it into a rule. Skills degrade silently in dynamic environments and need a loop: Observe -> Inspect -> Amend -> Evaluate. Cognee stores skills and memory in the same graph store; cognee.remember('skills/') ingests skills; a SkillChangeEvent emits memory events so a skill is a traceable, evolving memory node. 'If your memory system can't route a skill, it's not memory, let alone a world model.' Open-source. 56.8K views. agent-design, skills-mcp, research.

author: nader dabit
subject: Post by nader dabit on X
summary: 'Agent Hooks: Deterministic Control for Agent Workflows' — hooks attach handlers to lifecycle points (SessionStart, UserPromptSubmit, PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Stop, SessionEnd) so scripts/tests/policy run every time instead of relying on the model to remember. Operating model: event → matcher → handler → outcome. Rule of thumb: 'always/never/block/record/run/verify' belongs in a hook. Includes a demo and per-runtime notes (Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, Devin); layer prompts (guidance) with hooks (controls), CI, and human review.
content:
nader dabit @dabit3. X ARTICLE 'Agent Hooks: Deterministic Control for Agent Workflows.' Hooks attach user-defined handlers to lifecycle points so rules in scripts/tests/policy/runbooks run at known points instead of relying on the model to remember them. 'Use prompts for guidance; use hooks for behavior that should run every time.' Operating model: event -> optional matcher/filter -> handler -> outcome. Six core lifecycle points: SessionStart (load repo conventions/context), UserPromptSubmit (inspect/route/add context or block bad prompts), PreToolUse (block/approve/modify a tool call before it runs - e.g. protected paths generated/.env/.git, dangerous shell commands), PostToolUse (run tests/format/scan/log after a successful call, persist state), Stop (block completion until a condition like a passing quality gate is met), SessionEnd (final audit log/metrics/cleanup). Rule of thumb: when a requirement says 'always/never/block/record/run/verify' it belongs in a hook. Includes a checkout-calculator demo and per-runtime notes (Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, Devin). Layering: project instructions (style/architecture), hooks (context/policy/gates/logs), CI (independent verification), human review (judgment/risk). Adoption: start with one pre-action hook blocking protected-path edits, then a post-action quality gate + completion gate. 79.1K views. dev-practices, agent-design, claude-code.

Agent Hooks: Deterministic Control for Agent Workflows (full article)

content: Khairallah AL-Awady @eng_khairallah1. FULL COURSE 'How to Build a Team of AI Agents That Actually Work Together.' On May 6 2026 Anthropic announced multi-agent orchestration for Claude Managed Agents (up to 20 specialized agents in parallel; used by Netflix/Harvey/Shopify). Why multi-agent beats single: parallel speed + specialization. Three patterns: Pipeline (Research->Analysis->Writing->Review), Fan-Out (commander splits to N workers, e.g. Netflix build logs), Specialist Team (market/technical/financial/copy/review agents, e.g. Harvey legal). Steps: define team, design each agent (clear role, specific tools, defined/standardized output formats - most important technical decision), build orchestration (parallel vs sequential, data passing, failure fallback), add Memory with 'Dreaming' (scheduled background process reviewing sessions/extracting patterns/curating memory - Harvey saw ~6x completion-rate increase), define Outcomes (rubric-based self-grading), test simple first, monitor/iterate (handoff failures, redundant work, quality degradation, token bloat). Notes Anthropic's Cowork was built on this architecture and Apple will integrate Claude into iOS 27 via Extensions. 921.5K views. agent-design, claude-code, management.

author: Sam Hogan
subject: Post by Sam Hogan 🇺🇸 on X
summary: 'HALO: Using RLMs to build self-improving agents' - an agent that optimizes other agents by mining execution traces (via an RLM) for recurring harness failure modes and recommending fixes. Harness-only benchmark gains: Terminal-Bench 46->57%, Finance-Agent 56->72%, AppWorld up to 73.7->89.5%, SWE-Bench 65->74%; tau3-Bench exposed a model-capability (not harness) bottleneck.
content: Sam Hogan @samhogan. X ARTICLE 'HALO: Using RLMs to build self-improving agents.' Agent = model + harness; billions spent on models, less on harnesses. 'Mismanaged Genius Hypothesis' (a1zhang): LLMs are superhuman but humans manage harnesses poorly; let models shape the harness. HALO (Hierarchical Agent Loop Optimizer) is an agent that optimizes other agents by decomposing execution traces (via an RLM) across hundreds of thousands of runs to find recurring failure modes and recommend harness fixes applied by a coding agent, then re-run - a loop. Benchmark gains (harness-only): Terminal-Bench gemini-3-flash 46%->57% (Claude Code Opus 4.6 = 58%); fix was making the agent task-directed earlier instead of generic exploration. Finance-Agent opus-4-7 56%->72% via a coverage rubric for answer construction (didn't help GPT-5.5 as much; hurt Kimi-K2.6, which needed lighter instruction). AppWorld Sonnet 4.6 73.7%->89.5%, Gemini Flash 36.8%->52.6% via better tool recall/fallback/verification. SWE-Bench Gemini Flash 65%->74% via lightweight post-edit verification (over-constraining hurts). tau3-Bench 64%->67% but banking_knowledge capped ~10% = model-capability limit, not harness (useful negative result: HALO separates harness failures from model limits). GitHub: context-labs/halo. 23.7K views. agent-design, research, dev-practices.

subject: Post by Berryxia.AI on X
summary: Tencent AI open-sourced an agent-memory system after 6 months on context loss in long sessions: real-time compression of stale context (61% fewer tokens), a mermaid-based structured task map (fewer lost trajectories in 30+ step flows), and dedicated Persona memory (consistency 48->76%).
content: Berryxia.AI @berryxia (Chinese). Tencent AI spent 6 months on agents losing context in long sessions; open-sourced an agent-memory system (TencentDB-Agent-Memory). Three techniques: 1) real-time compression of stale context - cut token usage 61%; 2) structured task map generated in mermaid syntax - fewer lost trajectories in 30+ step flows (agent knows what step it's on); 3) dedicated Persona memory - personality consistency 48%->76%. Lesson: the hard part isn't storing more info, it's recalling the right thing the right way at the right time. QUOTE: Tencent AI announcing it. author: Petra Donka
subject: Post by Petra Donka on X
summary: 'Agents Need Feedback Loops, Not Perfect Prompts' - Warp's 'Buzz' monitors mentions and drafts replies while learning from the team. Lessons: principles beat rules; feedback isn't learning unless the agent generalizes (a separate skill turns corrections into transferable principles); the feedback loop must live where the team works (Slack emoji + daily PR); treat agent skills like code.
content: Petra Donka @petradonka. X ARTICLE 'Agents Need Feedback Loops, Not Perfect Prompts.' For judgement-heavy work the best prompt today won't be best in a month; question shifts from 'write the perfect prompt' to 'build agents that keep learning from the team after they ship.' Warp built 'Buzz' to monitor mentions across Twitter/Reddit/LinkedIn, decide reply/like/note/skip, and draft into Slack (humans still send). Lessons: (1) Principles beat rules - rules overfit and made replies brittle/robotic; principles (be helpful not defensive, check claims against docs, sound like a builder) made the skill smaller and better. (2) Feedback isn't learning unless the agent generalizes - a separate learning skill turns corrections into transferable principles (identify what went wrong -> why -> zoom to pattern -> check existing principles -> write as principle not rule -> place it right -> commit). (3) The feedback loop must fit where the team works - emoji reactions in Slack + optional notes; once daily Buzz compares its recs to what the team did, updates skill files, opens a PR. Treat agent skills like code (repo, version history, review, rollback). Runs ~15 skills, thousands of mentions/month, uses Oz for orchestration. Goal: compounding judgement. 99.7K views. agent-design, prompting, management.

content: ar0cket1 @ar0cket1. Deep technical research notes 'On Policy Self Distillation (OPSD).' Goal: RL-like upper bound with on-policy-distillation (OPD) sample efficiency and stability - a step toward viable continual learning (online RL is impractical, ~$10M for 155B tokens). Math on continual-learning cost (~$65 per 1M tokens of training, ~10x RL-efficient). Findings on Olmo 3 7B (Nemotron math v2): OPSD has much higher/more frequent KL shocks than OPD; OPSD tends to down-weight the student's chosen token (~83%) vs OPD up-weighting (~80%) - a stubborn 'positive pressure' gap; OPSD touches nearly opposite tokens to OPD. Tried GEPA evolutionary hint optimization (20+ hrs on RTX 6000 Pro) - reduced KL shocks (~1/2) but couldn't close the positive-pressure gap; a general hint-generation prompt beat per-problem optimization. Credits willccbb and nrehiew_. Seeking compute for full training runs. 53.7K views. research.

author: Rohit Ghumare
subject: Post by Rohit Ghumare on X
summary: On why agent architecture matters: codebases that survive past month six do so because the architecture makes the bad shape unexpressible (not team discipline). Four canonical month-six failures and the Worker/Function/Trigger 'React-of-2013' fix; cites the Claude Code April 2026 postmortem (runtime changes cut median thinking length 73%, pushed retries up 80x).
content: Rohit Ghumare @ghumare64. Most concrete piece on why agent architecture matters in production: agent codebases that survive past six months do so because the architecture makes the bad shape harder to write than the right one (not team discipline). Four canonical month-six failures (Mike Piccolo): class-level mutable defaults shared between agents once a second user shows up; tool functions accepting any string and returning None on failure; session memory mutated by an LLM-extracted string silently poisoning future actions; multi-agent passing the parent's full conversation history to a sub-agent. The 'React-of-2013 move': make discipline structural so the bug class becomes unexpressible. Worker/Function/Trigger: stateless worker invocations + namespaced memory worker (no shared Python state), typed input/output schemas validated at the boundary, sub-agents as workers with their own context (payload not conversation buffer), engine-level step bounds/timeouts. Cites the Claude Code April 2026 postmortem (three runtime changes, no model change, dropped median thinking length 73% and pushed retry rates up 80x). QUOTE ARTICLE: Mike Piccolo @mfpiccolo on month-six agent-codebase failures. 17.6K views. agent-design, dev-practices.

author: Jaynit
subject: Post by Jaynit on X
summary: A long essay on pattern recognition as the basis of expertise (de Groot's chess studies, chunking, Klein's recognition-primed decisions, Buffett reading annual reports), how tight feedback loops build it fast, and how to avoid false patterns. General/thinking, not AI/dev.
content: Jaynit @jaynitx. X ARTICLE 'pattern recognition: how to build the skill of seeing what others miss.' Expertise is better seeing, not better thinking: de Groot's chess studies (masters recall real positions far better but not random ones - they chunk patterns); Simon estimated masters store 50k-100k patterns. Chunking appears in reading, programming, investing, content. Building patterns needs time + deliberate practice, but especially tight feedback loops (Klein: weather forecasters develop intuition, clinicians often don't; Kahneman on quality/speed of feedback). Recognition-primed decision model: experts recognize-and-act rather than compare options (firefighters, Buffett reading annual reports 50 years). Watch for false patterns/narrative fallacy (Taleb); guard with feedback, sample size, multiple domains, mechanism. Speed it up via volume+variety, active analysis, immediate feedback, deliberate comparison, articulation, domain crossing. Limitation: patterns can become a prison (Kuhn paradigm shifts); hold them loosely. 494.1K views. general, management, prompting.

author: Joseph Viviano
subject: Post by Joseph Viviano on X
summary: 'Agentic Research Best Practices' - using coding agents for research (vs engineering): research code must be reproducible, understandable, correct, and fully understood, not production-grade. Workflow of paper.tex/design_doc.md -> roadmap -> task plans, short AGENT.md, separate planning/implementation/verification agents, notes.md, commit-per-TODO (git as savegames), worktrees, and constraining diffs. Reports 2-10x iteration speedups.
content: Joseph Viviano @josephdviviano (Mila). X ARTICLE 'Agentic Research Best Practices' - using coding agents for research (distinct from agentic engineering). Research codebases have no users: they must be portable/reproducible, easy to understand, correct (code/tests/docs/paper match), and fully understood by you; they need NOT be production-grade or full of abstractions. Reports 2-10x idea-iteration speedups. Workflow: high-level intent in paper.tex/design_doc.md -> roadmap.md -> task_plan.md; AGENT.md holds short universal rules (context rot is real; best performance 100-300k tokens); separate planning vs implementation agents; external verification by independent agents to avoid test-cheating; record discoveries in notes.md; commit each TODO separately (git as savegames); use worktrees for parallel agents/subagents. Emphasizes context management ('one bad prompt, one hundred bad lines'), tests/linting/types as constraints, having agents output scripts for risky ops (DB migrations), and anchoring the paper to the code via subagent discrepancy analysis. Tricks: warm-start from reference codebases/stubs, role-based one-off prompts (correctness reviewer, test designer, evaluator), file-structure-as-memory (small AGENT.md + skill pointers), constrain the diff, manage costs (plan with Opus, execute with Sonnet; stay <40% context). Postscript: practice by building a 3D game engine. 7,781 views. agent-design, dev-practices, research.

content: kloss @kloss_xyz. '/goal is the best command in Codex, Claude Code, and Hermes; most use it wrong (write "make no mistakes" and pray).' Full /goal prompt structure: GOAL (single measurable outcome), CONTEXT (repo/files/assumptions), CONSTRAINTS (what must not change, forbidden actions), PRIORITY, PLAN (understand first, restate understanding, minimal sufficient changes), DONE WHEN (verifiable completion), VERIFY (tests/build/lint/typecheck + rollback plan), OUTPUT (summary/changed files/risks/follow-ups), STOP RULES (halt on high-impact ambiguity, don't invent architecture, surface ranked proposals not open-ended questions, don't expand scope after goal met). 274.1K views. prompting, claude-code, dev-practices.
