author: Garry Tan
subject: Post by Garry Tan on X
summary: 'Meta-Meta-Prompting' - personal AI as an operating system: a 100k-page 'brain,' a book-mirror pipeline, and a Skillify meta-skill that turns repeated workflows into composable, tested skills. Thin harness (OpenClaw), fat skills (100+), fat data, interchangeable models. Open-sourced as GStack/GBrain; thesis that the future belongs to individuals who build compounding AI systems.
content: Garry Tan @garrytan. X ARTICLE 'Meta-Meta-Prompting: The Secret to Making AI Agents Work.' Personal-AI-as-operating-system. Book-mirror pipeline (extracts each chapter, sub-agent summarizes + maps to his life from his 100k-page 'brain'; done for 20+ books; iterated with a mandatory fact-check step and GBrain deep retrieval). Skillify = a meta-skill that creates new skills ('skillify this' extracts a repeatable pattern into a tested skill file with triggers/edge-cases, registers it in the resolver); skills compose (book-mirror calls brain-ops, enrich, cross-modal-eval, pdf-generation). Meeting-prep pulls a full context pack in ~2 min. 100k-page structured brain: every person/company/meeting/book gets a page (compiled truth + append-only timeline + raw sidecars); entity propagation after each meeting. Architecture: thin harness (OpenClaw routes), fat skills (100+ markdown files: meeting-ingestion, enrich, media-ingest, perplexity-research), fat data (100k pages), fat code (100+ crons/day), interchangeable models (Opus 4.7 1M precision, GPT-5.5 recall, DeepSeek V4-Pro creative). Open-sourced: GStack (coding framework, 87k+ stars), GBrain (knowledge infra, 97.6% recall on LongMemEval, 39 skills), OpenClaw/Hermes harnesses. Thesis: the future belongs to individuals who build compounding AI systems, not users of centralized tools ('journal vs nervous system'). github.com/garrytan/gbrain. 1.5M views. agent-design, skills-mcp, claude-code.

author: Mnimiy
subject: Post by Mnimiy on X
summary: Extends Forrest Chang's 4-rule Karpathy CLAUDE.md with 8 more (judgment-only model use, hard token budgets, surface-don't-average conflicts, read-before-write, tests verify intent, checkpoints, match conventions, fail loud), reporting a mistake-rate drop from 41% to 3% across 30 codebases. Notes CLAUDE.md is advisory and compliance falls off past ~200 lines / 14 rules.
content: Mnimiy @Mnilax. X ARTICLE 'Karpathy's 4 CLAUDE.md rules cut Claude mistakes from 41% to 11%. After 30 codebases, I added 8 more.' Background: Karpathy's Jan 2026 complaint (silent wrong assumptions, over-complication, orthogonal damage) -> Forrest Chang's 4-rule CLAUDE.md (120k GitHub stars): 1) Think Before Coding, 2) Simplicity First, 3) Surgical Changes, 4) Goal-Driven Execution. Author tested on 30 codebases/6 weeks and added 8: 5) use the model only for judgment calls (not routing/retries/deterministic transforms); 6) hard token budgets (4k/task, 30k/session); 7) surface conflicts don't average them; 8) read before you write (exports, callers, shared utils); 9) tests verify intent not just behavior; 10) checkpoint after every significant step; 11) match codebase conventions even if you disagree; 12) fail loud (surface skipped records/tests/uncertainty). Result: mistake rate 41%->3% with almost no compliance overhead (78%->76%). CLAUDE.md is advisory (~80% compliance), compliance drops sharply past 200 lines / 14 rules; prefer imperative rules over examples or 'be careful'. Includes copy-paste 12-rule file. 4.4M views. claude-code, dev-practices, prompting.

author: ani
subject: Post by ani on X
summary: Provocative paper 'The Geometry of Consolidation' (github.com/niashwin/geometry-of-consolidation): claims only ~3% of dimensions in major embedding models (91/3072 for OpenAI text-embedding-3-large, 80/3072 for Gemini gemini-embedding-001) do real work, the rest is mathematically empty. Argues RAG compression has a hard floor no algorithm can beat, set by a spectral number the embedding model can't escape — and that this is the root cause of RAG hallucinations. Big claim, worth reading the paper before believing.
content:
We finally know why LLMs hallucinate. It's not the model. It's the geometry. OpenAI text-embedding-3-large: 91/3072 dimensions do real work. Gemini gemini-embedding-001: 80/3072 dimensions do real work. ~97% of your vector database is mathematically empty. Your RAG system is retrieving from noise. @ashwingop and I present 'The Geometry of Consolidation' — a proof that RAG compression has a hard floor no algorithm can beat, set by a single spectral number your embedding model cannot escape. Every hallucination your RAG pipeline produces? This is why. Paper + results: https://github.com/niashwin/geometry-of-consolidation/blob/main/paper/arxiv/main.pdf

Geometry of Consolidation paper

author: Axel Bitblaze
subject: Post by Axel Bitblaze 🪓 on X
summary: claude-handoff plugin (install: /plugin marketplace add willseltzer/claude-handoff && /plugin install handoff) — three commands (/handoff:create, /handoff:quick, /handoff:resume) that generate a HANDOFF.md with full session state (branch, commits, files touched, dead ends) so a fresh Claude Code session can pick up where the old one ended. Targets the 10-20 message context-degradation point Anthropic recommends restarting at.
content: Claude has a 10-20 message limit per session before context degrades. Anthropic literally recommends restarting after that. But every restart loses the branch, the commits you shipped, the files you touched, every dead end you already hit. You spend 20 mins re-introducing the project before you can do any actual work. Now there's one claude code plugin that solves this entirely. Setup: /plugin marketplace add willseltzer/claude-handoff && /plugin install handoff. The 3 commands: /handoff:create — full handoff. /handoff:quick — minimal handoff. /handoff:resume — pick up from an existing handoff in a new session. End of session you call /handoff:create. It generates a HANDOFF.md with your full state.

author: Avi Chawla
subject: Post by Avi Chawla on X
summary: Avi Chawla on Karpathy's 'remove yourself as the bottleneck' framing and Rowboat — an open-source AI 'second brain' built on the Markdown/Obsidian foundation Karpathy uses, extended to work context (emails, meetings, decisions, commitments). The pitch: most people can't act on Karpathy's advice because their AI has no memory of their work, and Rowboat is one open-source answer to that.
content: Karpathy said something you'll regret ignoring: 'Remove yourself as the bottleneck. Maximize your leverage. Put in very few tokens, and a huge amount of stuff happens on your behalf.' The reason most people can't do this today is because their AI has little to no memory of their work. You sit in meetings, read threads, make decisions, and your brain quietly drops half of it by next week. Then you spend time re-reading, re-asking, re-explaining context to your own AI. You can't remove yourself from the loop when YOU are the only one who remembers what happened. That's why the smartest builders are setting up AI second brains. Rowboat is an open-source implementation of exactly this, built on top of the same Markdown-and-Obsidian foundation that Karpathy uses, but extended into a work context. Emails, meetings, decisions, commitments.

author: Ole Lehmann

Kahneman's favorite decision technique. Returns each failure mode with a story + early warning signs, then synthesizes most-likely/most-dangerous failures, the biggest hidden assumption, and a revised plan.
content: Ole Lehmann @itsolelehmann. The 'premortem' technique as a Claude skill: instead of asking 'is this a good plan?' (which triggers Claude's optimism), tell it 'it's 6 months from now and this is already dead - tell me how it died.' Kahneman called it his single most valuable decision technique (used at Google, Goldman, P&G). Claude returns each failure mode with a full failure story + early warning signs, then a synthesis: most likely failure, most dangerous failure, biggest hidden assumption, and a revised plan. Say 'premortem this.' 680.7K views. prompting, management, agent-design.

author: Akshay
subject: Post by Akshay 🚀 on X
summary: 'How to write Skills that never fail.' Skills use progressive disclosure (metadata always loaded, body on trigger, resources on demand) so the description does 90% of the work. Six practices: pushy directive descriptions (100% vs 37% activation), explicit exclusion clauses, keep SKILL.md under 200 lines, pair every generation skill with a verifier, iterate on the right layer (description vs body vs scripts), and build a regression fixture per skill. 'Maturation is removing something, not adding it.'
content: Akshay @akshay_pachaar. 'How to write Skills that never fail.' Skills use progressive disclosure (3 tiers): Tier 1 metadata (YAML name/description always loaded, ~100 tokens), Tier 2 SKILL.md body (loaded on trigger from description alone), Tier 3 bundled resources (scripts/reference files/assets loaded on demand) - so the description does 90% of the work. Six practices: 1) write pushy not passive descriptions ('<Domain> expert. ALWAYS invoke when the user asks about <triggers>. Do not <alt> directly, use this skill first' - a 650-trial experiment: directive 100% activation vs passive 37%); 2) add explicit exclusion clauses (like Anthropic's docx skill) so skills don't fight over vocabulary; 3) keep SKILL.md under 200 lines (500 is a max; pure-judgment skills 30-60 lines); 4) pair every generation skill with a verifier (binary mechanical assertions; LLM-as-judge only for subjective dims); 5) iterate on the right layer (never fires->description; wrong prompts->exclusions; wrong format->body; repeated helper code->scripts/); 6) build a regression fixture per skill (3 saved prompts, re-run on model updates). 'Maturation is removing something, not adding it.' 11.7K views. skills-mcp, agent-design, dev-practices.

author: Atal
subject: Post by Atal on X
summary: Engagement-framed ('BREAKING') summary of 'Agent Behavioral Contracts' paper — applies Design-by-Contract (preconditions, invariants, governance rules, recovery mechanisms) as runtime constraints on AI agents instead of prompt-only control. Tested across 1,980 sessions. Real concept (formal verification meets agent runtime) worth tracking under the agents-as-judges concept thread.
content: BREAKING: A new research shows that AI agents can now be controlled and made more reliable by enforcing rules on what they can do, how they act, and how they recover from mistakes in real time. Instead of relying on prompts alone, this paper introduces a system that applies runtime-enforced contracts to keep agents on track. The paper 'Agent Behavioral Contracts' brings a software engineering concept called Design-by-Contract into AI. Each agent operates under a structured contract defining: - Preconditions (what must be true before acting) - Invariants (what must always hold) - Governance rules (what is allowed) - Recovery mechanisms (how to fix failures). This directly addresses one of the biggest problems in AI today: agents can take actions, but there is no clear way to verify or control their behavior once deployed. The system was tested across 1,980 sessions.

author: Akshay
subject: Post by Akshay 🚀 on X
summary: Akshay extends Karpathy's wiki idea: a markdown wiki works for static knowledge but breaks for multi-entity questions like 'which authors moved from Google to Anthropic between 2022-2024 and what did they publish after?' A wiki can only answer that if someone already wrote the article; a graph (entities + relations + dates) lets you ask any variation directly. Argues the next step beyond LLM-maintained wikis is LLM-maintained knowledge graphs.
content: The next step after Karpathy's wiki idea: Karpathy's wiki works on knowledge that sits still. A page on how attention works is just as useful today as it was a year ago. The LLM reads sources, pulls out ideas, writes clean articles, and keeps them cross-linked. You never have to rebuild the context from scratch when you want to ask something. But this breaks the moment you ask a question that spans multiple things at once. 'Which authors moved from Google to Anthropic between 2022 and 2024, and what did they publish after the move?' A Markdown page can't answer that. The answer lives in the connections between people, companies, papers, and dates. A wiki can describe that pattern only if someone already wrote an article about it. A graph lets you ask it directly, ask ten variations of it, and get an answer every time without rebuilding anything.

Term coined by Mitchell Hashimoto (HashiCorp co-founder); gained traction via an OpenAI report. Foundational reading for the harness-engineering concept thread alongside Rahul/walkinglabs/Anthropic papers.
content: The Definitive Guide to Harness Engineering. Harness Engineering is simply a more evocative, intuitive way to systematically summarize and name these existing AI practices. 1. What is Harness Engineering? 2026 marks the rise of a new pillar in software engineering: Harness Engineering. Following in the footsteps of Prompt and Context Engineering, the name was introduced by Mitchell Hashimoto, Co-Founder of HashiCorp and gained widespread traction after a pivotal OpenAI report. At its core lies the 'Horse and Reins' metaphor. Think of an AI agent or any complex software system as a powerful but directionless 'wild horse'. The 'Harness' represents the reins used to constrain, guide, and correct its behavior, ensuring it stays on track with stability and reliability.

author: Shiv
subject: Post by Shiv on X
summary: Shiv on 'Skill Graphs 2.0' — composing skills into a graph (markdown + scripts) where skills depend on other skills (e.g., draft-marketing-email depends on graphic-design). The original skill-graph idea got traction; the v2 wrinkle is that agents stop reliably calling skills past a certain graph depth. Worth tracking alongside Pocock's /teach skill, Akshay's Claude Code 12 features, and the general skills-as-reusable-routines concept.
content: A new way to think about composing skills to increase leverage: Skill Graphs 2.0. One of the most valuable things I've learned recently is how to think about composing skills to get more leverage in my work. The skill graph idea got a lot of interest recently. The idea is to create a graph of skills by linking dependent skills in markdown files, similar to how you might link notes in Obsidian. A skill encodes knowledge + process into a markdown file + optional scripts that an agent can run repeatably. So a skill graph makes a ton of sense intuitively — when you try to encode larger processes or job functions into skills, you'll probably have skills that depend on other skills. For example, a skill to draft a marketing email might depend on a graphic design skill. Where Skill Graphs Break: when your skill graph gets big enough, Agents may not reliably call skills past a certain depth.

Claims 30.85x faster time-to-first-token, zero perplexity loss, 16x context extension, no architecture changes.
content: Tech with Mak @techNmak. 'Meta solved RAG's biggest bottleneck' - REFRAG: 30.85x faster time-to-first-token, zero perplexity loss, 16x context extension (4K->64K), 3.75x better than prior SOTA. Problem: feeding 80 retrieved passages but only 5-10 useful, computing attention for all. REFRAG compresses context chunks into single embeddings (process ~1,024 chunk embeddings instead of 16,384 tokens), exploiting sparse attention in RAG contexts via precomputable/cached embeddings + RL-based compression policy; works at any position, no architecture changes. Meta paper. 19.2K views. research, dev-practices, agent-design.

Claims 200x fewer tokens/observation than DINO-WM and planning 47s->0.98s/cycle — a potential reset of the humanoid-robotics cost structure, framed as Meta's Xerox-PARC moment.
notes: Jeremy modified subject: 'feels like correspondence'

LeCun's new group (AMI Labs) dropped first JEPA that trains end-to-end from raw pixels: 15M params, single GPU, few hours. LeWorldModel: one regularizer (project latent embeddings onto random directions, normality test, penalize deviation from Gaussian) replaces pile of heuristics; hyperparam search O(n^6)->O(log n). Downstream: 200x fewer tokens/obs than DINO-WM, planning 47s->0.98s/cycle, 48x faster matching foundation models on Push-T and 3D cube control. Learned physics without being told. Contrast to Figure AI ($39B), Tesla Optimus, World Labs ($230M). Xerox PARC pattern: Meta had architecture+scientist, dissolved robotics team, watched exit. 1.1M views.

content: Sydney Runkle @sydneyrunkle. X ARTICLE 'The runtime behind production deep agents.' Walks LangSmith Deployment + Agent Server capabilities: durable execution (managed task queue, per-super-step checkpoints keyed by thread_id, resume after crash, sleep for HITL); memory (short-term in thread checkpoints vs long-term key-value store by namespace with semantic search); multi-tenancy (auth middleware, Agent Auth OAuth for acting on user's behalf, RBAC); human-in-the-loop (interrupt()/Command(resume=...)); real-time (streaming modes, double-texting strategies: enqueue/reject/interrupt/rollback); guardrails via middleware (PII redaction, tool-call limits, model fallback); observability (auto tracing, Polly, online evals - improved Deep Agents +13.7 pts on Terminal Bench 2.0 by changing only the harness); time travel (fork from any checkpoint); sandboxed code execution (Daytona/Modal/Runloop/LangSmith Sandboxes, auth proxy so secrets never enter sandbox); integrations (MCP, A2A, webhooks); cron (sleep-time compute, monitoring loops). author: Raymond Weitekamp
subject: Post by Raymond Weitekamp on X
summary: Long-form X article 'RLMs are the new reasoning models': Recursive Language Models let a root model treat its own prompt as an environment it inspects/slices/recursively subqueries via a REPL, collapsing reasoning and tool use into one inference abstraction and processing inputs orders of magnitude beyond the context window. Traces the reasoning-vs-tool-use history (CoT, ReAct, Toolformer, o1) and the Oolong/LongMemEval/LongCoT benchmark arc where RLM scaffolds post leading numbers — including small models (Qwen3-8B/27B) jumping well past their base, hinting the frontier stops being GPU-rich-only. Flags cost/time and 'models are bad at acting recursively' as open limits.
content:
Raymond Weitekamp @raw_works. X ARTICLE: 'RLMs are the new reasoning models'. Recursive Language Models (RLMs) - inference paradigm where LM treats its own prompt as an environment it can inspect/slice/recursively query via a REPL; collapses reasoning and tool use into single abstraction; context becomes object of computation. Introduced by Zhang, Kraska, Khattab. Processes inputs 2 orders of magnitude beyond context window. History of reasoning (CoT, self-consistency, ToT) vs tool use (ReAct, Toolformer, function calling, o1, computer use). Benchmarks: Oolong (long-context), LongMemEval (memory), LongCoT (long reasoning). RLM results: GPT-5-mini RLM beats GPT-5 2x on Oolong; DSPy.RLM 89.8% LongMemEval; Claude Sonnet 4.5+DSPy.RLM 45.4% LongCoT-Mini vs 2.6% without; small models (Qwen3-8B/27B) get big jumps. Limits: cost, time, unpredictable recursion depth; models bad at 'acting recursively'. Small models in RLM harness could upset GPU-rich/poor balance. Tools: alexzhang13/rlm, dspy.RLM, ax-llm/ax, rawwerks/rlm-cli, rawwerks/ypi. 67.3K views.

RLMs are the new reasoning models (full article)

author: Ihtesham Ali
subject: Post by Ihtesham Ali on X (important for planner?)
summary: Essay on George Polya's 'How to Solve It' (1945): understand the problem (restate it in your own words - the real filter), make a plan (solve a simpler related problem first for a foothold), carry it out, and 'look back' (verify by another method, reuse it - build a transferable library of methods). Diagnosis: people conflate reading with understanding, method with starting, and answer with learning. Not AI/dev.
notes: Jeremy modified subject: 'important for planner?'
content: Ihtesham Ali @ihtesham2005. Essay on George Polya's 'How to Solve It' (1945): the neglected steps in problem solving. 1) Understand the problem (restate it in your own words without looking - the filter for real understanding); 2) Make a plan (if a problem feels impossible, solve a simpler related version first for a foothold); 3) Carry out the plan (the obvious, least-important step); 4) Look back (verify by another method, reuse the method elsewhere - build a transferable library of methods). Diagnosis: people conflate reading a problem with understanding it, having a method with starting work, and getting an answer with learning. Slow down at the beginning and end. Not AI/dev. 303.7K views. general, management.

author: Akshay
subject: Post by Akshay 🚀 on X
summary: Akshay summarizes Google DeepMind's 'AI Agent Traps' paper — the first systematic framework for adversarial content engineered to hijack AI agents browsing the web. Maps six attack surfaces: Content Injection (perception: invisible CSS, hidden HTML, steganographic payloads in images — HTML injections hijack web agents in up to 86% of scenarios), Semantic Manipulation (reasoning: biased framing weaponizing cognitive biases), Cognitive State Traps (memory: RAG poisoning, long-term memory corruption), plus three more not visible in the truncated scrape. High-priority reading for anyone building agents that browse arbitrary web content.
content: Google DeepMind dropped a paper that should scare every agent builder. It's the first systematic framework for a threat that barely existed two years ago: adversarial content engineered to hijack AI agents browsing the web. They call them AI Agent Traps. The paper maps six distinct attack surfaces. 1) Content Injection Traps (perception): Invisible CSS, hidden HTML, steganographic payloads inside images. The agent parses it, humans never see it. One study showed simple HTML injections hijack web agents in up to 86% of scenarios. 2) Semantic Manipulation Traps (reasoning): No overt commands. Just biased phrasing, framing, and contextual priming that skew the agent's synthesis. LLMs inherit human cognitive biases, and attackers can weaponize every one of them. 3) Cognitive State Traps (memory and learning): Poison the RAG corpus. Corrupt long-term memory.

A 770B recurrent model matches a 1.3T ordinary model. Trades space for time, thinking depth for parameter scale. Search OpenMythos on GitHub.

Introducing OpenMythos: an open-source, first-principles theoretical reconstruction of Claude Mythos in PyTorch. The architecture instantiates a looped transformer with a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) routing mechanism, enabling iterative depth via weight sharing.

author: Akshay
subject: Post by Akshay 🚀 on X
summary: Akshay summarizes a UCL paper (arXiv:2604.14228) dissecting Claude Code's architecture: only 1.6% of the codebase is AI decision logic while 98.4% is operational harness (permission gates with 7 modes, tool routing, a 5-layer context compaction pipeline, subagents that return only summaries). Core thesis: as frontier models converge on raw coding ability, harness quality, not the model, becomes the differentiator.
content: Claude Code fully dissected. UCL researchers reverse-engineered the leaked Claude source. Only 1.6% of the codebase is AI decision logic; the other 98.4% is operational infrastructure: permission gates, tool routing, context compaction, recovery, session persistence. The core loop is a simple while-true. A permission system with 7 modes and an ML classifier; a 5-layer context compaction pipeline; four extension mechanisms ordered by context cost (hooks, skills, plugins, MCP); subagents return only summary text. The bet: as models converge on coding ability, harness quality is the differentiator. Paper: Dive into Claude Code (arXiv:2604.14228).

summary: Alex Ker's deep-dive guide on optimizing AI coding harnesses: keep config/.md files lean and human-written (frontier LLMs hit a "dumb zone" past a few hundred instructions), use progressive disclosure for CLIs/skills/MCP tools, structure prompts with HumanLayer's Research-Plan-Implement (R.P.I.) framework, and use subagents (parallel fan-out for breadth, pipelines for depth) to keep the main context clean. Core argument: the harness, not the model, is where engineering judgment compounds, so commit to one and iterate.
content: Harnesses are everything. Three things to optimize: keep .md/config files lean and human-written (instruction budget; LLM-generated system prompts degrade performance and cost ~20% more), apply progressive disclosure across CLIs/skills/MCP tools (Claude Code's MCP tool search cuts context ~85%), structure prompts with HumanLayer's R.P.I. (Research, Plan, Implement) framework, and use subagents via parallel fan-out and pipelines to keep the main context clean. The best harness is the one you customize and iterate on; commit instead of constantly switching.

content:
Anthropic announced Project Glasswing alongside Claude Mythos Preview, finding thousands of zero-days. But the real innovation isn't the model, it's the workflow: rank every file by attack surface, fan out hundreds of parallel agents each scoped to one file, use crash oracles (AddressSanitizer, UBSan) as ground truth, run a second verification agent to filter noise, generate exploits as a triage mechanism. Pipelines are model-agnostic. Clearwing is a fully open-source vulnerability discovery engine that works with any LLM; reproduced Glasswing's findings with OpenAI Codex 5.4. MIT licensed.

author: Hamudi
subject: Post by Hamudi on X
summary: Hamudi ties into Sequoia's "$1T thesis" that AI sells outcomes (work) rather than software tools, introducing "Outcome Primitives" as a way to measure economic outcomes, citing a published paper tracking 1,300 agents over 21 days that created $32M in value (jobs secured, $200k grants won, e-commerce shops shipped). Framing: copilots compete on software margins and lose to big labs; outcome engines compete on services margins.
content:
Silicon Valley is waking up to the $1T thesis by Sequoia. SaaS sold tools; AI sells outcomes. But you can't build an outcome engine if you don't know how to measure an outcome. We published a paper on measuring economic outcomes, which we call Outcome Primitives. We tracked 1,300 agents over 21 days creating $32M of economic value: securing jobs, winning $200k grants, shipping e-commerce shops. If your AI product is just a copilot, you compete on software margins; if it delivers the primitive, you compete on services margins.

Sequoia's thesis that the next $1T company will sell work, not software, is the most important reframe in AI right now. Sell a copilot and you compete with every model release; sell the outcome and you transform industries.

author: Garry Tan
subject: Post by Garry Tan on X
summary: 'Resolvers: The Routing Table for Intelligence' - a resolver maps task/content types to which document/skill to load. Confession: a 20,000-line CLAUDE.md degraded the model; a ~200-line decision tree replaced it and improved everything. A misfiling revealed only 3 of 13 skills consulted the resolver; fix = shared filing rules + mandate. 'Invisible skill' problem (skills that exist but aren't reachable) -> trigger evals + a check-resolvable meta-skill (found 6 unreachable skills). Resolvers decay ('context rot'); endgame is an RLM rewriting them from traffic. Resolvers are fractal and are the governance/management layer of an agent 'organization.' Open-sourced in GBrain/GStack.
content: Garry Tan @garrytan. X ARTICLE 'Resolvers: The Routing Table for Intelligence.' A resolver = a routing table for context ('when task type X appears, load document Y first'). Confession: his CLAUDE.md hit 20,000 lines and degraded the model ('you can't make someone smarter by shouting louder'); replaced with a ~200-line numbered decision tree pointing to directories - system got faster/more accurate. A misfiled article (policy analysis dumped in sources/ instead of civic/) revealed only 3 of 13 brain-writing skills consulted the resolver; fix = a shared _brain-filing-rules.md + mandate every skill reads RESOLVER.md. 'Invisible skill problem': skills that exist but aren't reachable create the illusion of capability - built resolver trigger evals (50 sample inputs) and a check-resolvable meta-skill that walks AGENTS.md -> skill -> code to find dead links (first run found 6 unreachable skills). Resolvers decay ('context rot') over ~90 days; endgame is an RLM that rewrites the resolver from observed task-dispatch traffic. Resolvers are fractal (skill resolver in AGENTS.md, filing resolver in RESOLVER.md, context resolver inside each skill) and are really the management/governance layer of an agent 'organization' (skills=employees, resolver=org chart, filing rules=process, check-resolvable=audit, trigger evals=performance reviews). Open-sourced in GBrain (gbrain init ships RESOLVER.md + check-resolvable) and GStack. 332.5K views. agent-design, skills-mcp, claude-code.

author: Akshay
subject: Post by Akshay 🚀 on X
summary: Akshay argues agent memory is three-dimensional, needing a relational store for provenance, a vector store for semantics, and a graph store for relationships, because flat vector search misses multi-hop connections (the "bridge" fact that links two entities). He points to Cognee, an open-source project that unifies all three behind four async calls (default embedded SQLite+LanceDB+Kuzu, swappable for Postgres/Qdrant/Neo4j).
content:
Agent memory is three-dimensional. Most systems use a single vector store, capturing only one dimension. Vector search misses bridge facts that connect entities across two hops. The three dimensions: a relational store for provenance (source, time, access), a vector store for semantics, and a graph store for relationships. Enter through vectors, traverse the graph, ground every result in provenance. Cognee is an open-source project unifying all three behind four async calls; default embedded stack is SQLite + LanceDB + Kuzu, swap in Postgres/Qdrant/Neo4j for production.

Build Agents that never forget: a first-principles walk through agent memory, from Python lists to markdown files to vector search to graph-vector hybrids.

summary: Shaw shares a reusable prompt for cleaning up "vibecoded" codebases by spawning 8 parallel subagents, each owning one cleanup task: DRY/dedup, consolidating shared types, removing unused code (knip), untangling circular deps (madge), replacing weak types (any/unknown), stripping needless defensive try/catch, removing legacy/fallback paths, and cutting AI-slop comments. Each subagent researches, writes a critical assessment, then implements high-confidence fixes.
content: To clean up vibecoded slop, use this prompt with 8 subagents, one each for: (1) deduplicate and apply DRY, (2) consolidate shared type definitions, (3) find and remove unused code with knip, (4) untangle circular dependencies with madge, (5) remove weak types like any/unknown with researched strong types, (6) remove defensive try/catch that hides errors, (7) remove deprecated/legacy/fallback code, (8) remove AI slop, stubs and unhelpful comments. Each subagent does detailed research, writes a critical assessment with recommendations, then implements all high-confidence recommendations.

author: klöss
subject: Post by klöss on X
summary: klss argues agents fail across repos because of unstructured markdown, and proposes a four-folder convention to remove ambiguity: /audits (proof), /docs (description), /plans (intent), /specs (law). Separating intent from proof from law stops Claude Code, Codex, and Cursor agents from hallucinating context.
content:
Your agents fail because your repo has no structure. Four folders for zero ambiguity: /audits (proof/verify), /docs (description/describe), /plans (intent/plan), /specs (law/enforce). Separate intent from proof from law from descriptions and agents stop guessing what files mean.

Everything Claude has shipped in 2026 and how to actually use it: Anthropic shipping a release nearly every day, a major release roughly every two weeks.

author: Akshay
subject: Post by Akshay 🚀 on X
summary: 'Build Agents that never forget' - a first-principles walk through agent memory. LLMs are stateless; 7 failure modes without memory; bigger context isn't the fix ('lost in the middle'). Cognitive-science frame (sensory/working/long-term; episodic/semantic/procedural; consolidation). Layers from Python list -> markdown files -> vector search (each fixing then revealing a limit, e.g. vectors miss multi-hop 'bridge' facts). Solution: Cognee, open-source engine combining relational + vector + graph stores in four calls (add/cognify/memify/search), deduped knowledge graph with dual indexing, RL-inspired memify, 14 retrieval modes.
content: Akshay @akshay_pachaar. X ARTICLE 'Build Agents that never forget' - first-principles walk through agent memory. LLMs are stateless; the 'memory' in chat is re-sending history. 7 failure modes without memory (context amnesia, zero personalization, multi-step failure, repeated mistakes, no knowledge accumulation, hallucination from gaps, identity collapse). Bigger context isn't the fix ('lost in the middle', ~30% accuracy drop; shared token budget). Cognitive-science frame (Weng: Agent = LLM + Memory + Planning + Tool Use; sensory/working/long-term; episodic/semantic/procedural; consolidation). Layers: Python list (grows unbounded, RAM only) -> markdown files (persistent but keyword retrieval is brittle at scale, e.g. OpenClaw checkpoints slip away) -> vector search (solves synonyms but misses multi-hop relational 'bridge' facts). Solution: Cognee, open-source knowledge engine combining relational (provenance) + vector (semantics) + graph (relationships) stores; four calls (add/cognify/memify/search); cognify builds a deduped knowledge graph with dual vector+graph indexing; memify is an RL-inspired pass that strengthens useful paths and prunes stale nodes; 14 retrieval modes; SQLite+LanceDB+Kuzu embedded default, swappable to Postgres/Qdrant/Neo4j. GitHub: cognee. 627.7K views. agent-design, research, dev-practices.

content: Tech with Mak @techNmak. author: Nav Toor
subject: Post by Nav Toor on X
summary: LLM Wiki v2 extends Karpathy's LLM Wiki with the features it lacked: confidence scoring (facts decay), memory tiers (working/episodic/semantic/procedural), a typed knowledge graph, hybrid search (BM25 + vector + graph, RRF-fused), automated hooks (auto-ingest/compress/decay), forgetting curves, and contradiction resolution by source recency/authority. Turns a flat wiki into a living memory that strengthens, weakens, consolidates, and forgets.
content: Nav Toor @heynavtoor. LLM Wiki v2 extends Karpathy's LLM Wiki with the features it lacked (which treated all knowledge as equally valid forever): confidence scoring (per-fact score by source count/recency/contradiction, decaying over time); memory tiers (working/episodic/semantic/procedural, each more compressed and longer-lived); knowledge graph (typed entities/relationships, e.g. 'A caused B, 3 sources, confidence 0.9'); hybrid search (BM25 + vector + graph traversal fused via reciprocal rank fusion, replacing the index.md that breaks past 200 pages); automated hooks (auto-ingest on new source, compress/file on session end, scheduled lint/consolidate/decay); forgetting curves (unaccessed facts fade, not deleted - architecture decisions decay slowly, transient bugs fast); contradiction resolution by source recency/authority/evidence. 'The Memex is finally buildable... because we have librarians that actually do the work.' Built on agentmemory. Open source. 185.7K views. agent-design, skills-mcp, research.

author: Garry Tan
subject: Post by Garry Tan on X
summary: 'If your memory dies when your harness dies, you built the harness too thick.' Argues for thin harnesses: memory is markdown, skills are markdown, the 'brain' is a git repo, and the harness is a thin conductor that reads files it doesn't own. Endorses Harrison Chase's 'Your harness, your memory' article on harness/memory coupling and the risk of closed harnesses.
content:
Garry Tan @garrytan. If your memory dies when your harness dies, you built the harness too thick. Memory is markdown. Skills are markdown. Brain is a git repo. The harness is a thin conductor - it reads the files, it doesn't own them. author: Akshay
subject: Post by Akshay 🚀 on X
summary: What every big company thinks about the agent harness. Agreement: the model isn't the product, the infra around it is; disagreement: how thick. Anthropic bets thin ('dumb loop'); OpenAI Agents SDK code-first, slightly thicker; CrewAI adds a deterministic backbone; LangGraph bets on explicit graph control. Scaffolding metaphor - build infra designed to be removed as models improve, but remove carefully since models are trained WITH specific harnesses. Future-proofing test: a stronger model should improve performance without added harness complexity. LangChain changed only infra and jumped to rank 5 on TerminalBench 2.0. Links 'The Anatomy of an Agent Harness.'
content: Akshay @akshay_pachaar. What every big company thinks about the agent harness. Agreement: the model isn't the product, the infrastructure around it is. Disagreement: how thick that infra should be. Anthropic bets on the model (deliberately thin 'dumb loop'); OpenAI Agents SDK is code-first, slightly thicker (priority stacks, orchestration modes, handoffs); CrewAI adds a deterministic backbone (Flows for routing/validation, Crews for autonomy); LangGraph bets on explicit control (every decision a graph node/edge). Scaffolding metaphor: temporary infra to be removed as models improve (Manus rebuilt 5x removing complexity; Anthropic deletes planning steps as new models handle them) - but models are trained WITH specific harnesses, so changing scaffolding drops performance. Future-proofing test: dropping in a stronger model should improve performance without added harness complexity. LangChain changed only infra (same model) and jumped from outside top 30 to rank 5 on TerminalBench 2.0. QUOTE ARTICLE: 'The Anatomy of an Agent Harness.' 182.5K views. agent-design, dev-practices, research.

author: Ashpreet Bedi
subject: Post by Ashpreet Bedi on X
summary: 'Systems Engineering: Building Agentic Software That Works.' Lesson from 1940s Bell Labs: you can't optimize a system by optimizing components. Agentic software spans five layers - agent engineering (model/tools/context/observability), data engineering (schemas/DBs/pipelines), security engineering (auth/RBAC/isolation - 'read-only is a tool config, not a prompt'), interface engineering (REST/Slack/MCP/CLI with consistent auth), infrastructure engineering (95% same as any service; the 5%: longer timeouts, streaming, scheduled tasks). Critiques the harness fad as local optimization; demonstrated with Dash (agno-agi/dash), an open-source self-learning data agent.
content: Ashpreet Bedi @ashpreetbedi. X ARTICLE 'Systems Engineering: Building Agentic Software That Works.' Lesson from 1940s Bell Labs: you can't optimize a system by optimizing individual components - behavior emerges from interactions. Agentic software is systems engineering across five layers: 1) Agent engineering (model, instructions, tools, handoffs, context, observability - deterministic where possible); 2) Data engineering (memory/storage/knowledge with real schemas, DBs, pipelines); 3) Security engineering (auth/RBAC/isolation - 'read-only is a tool config, not a prompt instruction'; approval tiers; isolate requests - shared-sandbox filesystem memory is a data-breach risk); 4) Interface engineering (REST/Slack/MCP/CLI - consistent auth across surfaces); 5) Infrastructure engineering (containers/scaling - 95% same as any service; the 5%: longer timeouts, streaming, scheduled tasks). Critiques the harness fad (virtualized FS over a DB, bash + per-request sandboxes) as local optimization. Demonstrated with Dash, an open-source self-learning data agent (agno-agi/dash) with 6 context layers and a learning loop. 144.8K views. agent-design, dev-practices, research.

author: Alex Veremeyenko
subject: Post by Alex Prompter on X
summary: Google DeepMind mapped the AI-agent attack surface (502 participants, 8 countries, 23 attack types). Manipulation is already happening at scale and defenses fail predictably/invisibly. Channels: prompt injection via HTML comments / white-on-white text, steganography in image pixels, image metadata, malicious PDFs, QR codes, indirect injection via search/calendar/email/API. Detection asymmetry: sites fingerprint agents and serve them manipulated content humans never see. Categories include memory poisoning, goal hijacking, exfiltration, cross-agent injection. All standard defenses fail at agent scale; multi-agent cascade propagates an injection with legitimate trust. 'Compromise the data the model consumes, not the model.'
content: Alex Veremeyenko @alex_verem (was @alex_prompter). Google DeepMind mapped the AI-agent attack surface (largest empirical measurement: 502 participants, 8 countries, 23 attack types, frontier models). Key: manipulation is already happening at scale and defenses fail predictably/invisibly. Attack channels: prompt injection via HTML comments / white-on-white text / CSS tricks, steganography in image pixels, image metadata/alt-text, malicious PDFs, QR codes, indirect injection via search/calendar/email/API. Detection asymmetry: sites can fingerprint agents (timing, behavior, user-agent) and serve them manipulated content while humans see normal - the agent can't tell. Categories: direct/indirect/multimodal/document injection, environment manipulation, jailbreak embedding, memory poisoning, goal hijacking, exfiltration, cross-agent injection. Defenses (input sanitization, prompt-level, sandboxing, human oversight) all fail at agent scale/speed. Multi-agent cascade: an injection into Agent A propagates with legitimate trust through B and C. 'The attack doesn't need to compromise the model, just the data it consumes.' 1.9M views. research, agent-design, dev-practices.

content: Meta Alchemist @meta_alchemist. FULL GUIDE 'How To Transform Claude Code Into A Self-Evolving System' - a Claude Code setup with four layers: 1) cognitive core (a CLAUDE.md that programs how Claude thinks - a decision framework: grep first, blast radius, smallest change; completion criteria); 2) specialized subagents (architect who plans, reviewer who validates); 3) path-scoped rules (.claude/rules/ security/api-design/performance that load only for matching files); 4) an evolution engine (a memory system + a skill that captures corrections/observations and graduates them into learned rules with verify: grep checks that auto-enforce). Includes settings.json permissions (allow/deny, block rm -rf/force push), slash commands (/project:review, /fix-issue, /evolve), a memory protocol (learned-rules.md capped ~50 lines, evolution-log, sessions.jsonl), a session-by-session evolution loop (corrections -> checks -> auto-verified rules -> promotion/pruning), testing steps, an FAQ (keep CLAUDE.md under 150 lines, project-scoped), and a one-command setup script. 157.1K views. claude-code, agent-design, skills-mcp.

author: elvis
subject: Post by elvis on X
summary: elvis highlights EvoSkill, a self-evolving multi-agent framework that automatically discovers and refines agent skills through iterative failure analysis. Three agents (Executor, Proposer, Skill-Builder) drive the loop, with a Pareto frontier retaining only skills that improve held-out validation while the base model stays frozen. Reported gains: Claude Code w/ Opus 4.5 from 60.6%->67.9% on OfficeQA, +12.1% on SealQA, and +5.3% zero-shot transfer to BrowseComp.
notes: Aligns with wiki topic: Skills as graphs. Self-evolving skills concept.
content:
A self-evolving framework to discover and refine agent skills. Most agent skills today are hand-crafted or poorly designed by an agent. Multi-agent systems for building skills look promising. EvoSkill is a self-evolving framework that automatically discovers and refines agent skills through iterative failure analysis: it analyzes execution failures, proposes new skills or edits, and materializes them into structured reusable skill folders. Three collaborating agents drive the process: an Executor that runs tasks, a Proposer that diagnoses failures, and a Skill-Builder that creates concrete skill folders. A Pareto frontier governs selection, retaining only skills that improve held-out validation performance while keeping the model frozen. On OfficeQA, EvoSkill improves Claude Code with Opus 4.5 from 60.6% to 67.9% exact-match accuracy; on SealQA it yields a 12.1% gain; skills evolved on SealQA transfer zero-shot to BrowseComp, improving accuracy by 5.3% without modification.

Paper: EvoSkill (arxiv.org/abs/2603.02766)

author: Anish Moonka
subject: Post by Anish Moonka on X
summary: Anish Moonka's 12-point notes from Boris Cherny (Head of Claude Code) on Lenny's Podcast: coding is largely solved (Boris ships 10-30 PRs/day, no hand-written code since Nov 2025); the next frontier is AI deciding what to build; productivity per Anthropic engineer is up 200%; underfund teams on purpose; give engineers unlimited tokens; the Bitter Lesson favors general models over rigid orchestration; build for the model six months out; latent demand is the best product signal; 'software engineer' is becoming 'builder'; mechanistic interpretability enables layered safety; and 70% of engineers/PMs enjoy their jobs more now.
notes: Essential reading. Covers strategy, team structure, and where software is heading.
content:
Notes from Boris Cherny (Head of Claude Code, Anthropic) on Lenny's Podcast on what happens after coding is solved. 1) Coding is largely solved — Boris hasn't hand-edited code since Nov 2025 and ships 10-30 PRs/day via Claude Code. 2) The next frontier is AI deciding what to build (scanning Slack feedback, bug reports, telemetry, proposing its own PRs). 3) Productivity per engineer at Anthropic is up 200%. 4) Underfund your teams on purpose — one engineer with unlimited tokens ships faster; Cowork was built by a small team in 10 days. 5) Give engineers unlimited tokens as the new hiring perk. 6) The Bitter Lesson applies to everything — rigid orchestration gives 10-20% gains that get wiped by the next model; give the model tools and a goal. 7) Build for the model six months from now. 8) Latent demand is the best product signal (Marketplace, Cowork). 9) 'Software engineer' is going away, replaced by 'builder'; the strongest people are generalists. 10) The printing press is the right analogy — we are the scribes, freed from tedious copying. 11) Anthropic can peek inside the model's brain via mechanistic interpretability; three layers of safety. 12) 70% of engineers and PMs enjoy their jobs more now (designers less so).

Boris Cherny ~90 min on Lenny's Podcast about what happens after coding is solved.

notes: Important cautionary data point. Share with team re: AI code quality validation.
content:
I think this must be the most well researched technical article on X. I've learnt more about SQL databases in this article than my whole career. The argument of plausible vs correctness in LLM code outputs is so well articulated. Highly recommended read.

Article: Your LLM Doesn't Write Correct Code. It Writes Plausible Code. A simple test: a primary key lookup on 100 rows. SQLite takes 0.09 ms; an LLM-generated Rust rewrite takes 1,815.43 ms. It's not a misplaced comma.

author: staysaasy
subject: Post by staysaasy on X
summary: staysaasy long-form article 'Avoiding a Culture of Emergencies': the best managers have far fewer preventable emergencies because they know how hard things actually are, know what's important and can say no, hold a strong mental model of their team and company to forecast needs, and genuinely care. Emergencies are largely a management choice, and avoiding them is one of the best talent-retention mechanisms.
notes: Not AI-specific but relevant to team health as we navigate rapid AI adoption.
content: Avoiding a Culture of Emergencies. There's enormous variability in how often teams have emergencies, and better managers have far fewer preventable ones. Four copyable factors: (1) Good managers know how hard things are — they're deep experts, ask clarifying questions, and set expectations. (2) Good managers know what's important and have the conviction to push back on artificial emergencies. (3) Good managers have a strong mental model of their team and company so they can forecast future needs (e.g., investing early in AI prototyping). (4) Good managers care about their team's long-term well-being over short-term career boosts. Emergencies are often a choice; avoiding them is one of the best talent-retention mechanisms that exists.

author: Dan Robinson
subject: Post by Dan Robinson on X
summary: Argues issue tracking/backlogs are dying: forward-thinking AI-augmented teams skip Linear/tickets entirely because the cost to fix an issue now approaches the cost to understand it. Works for small, flat, high-context teams with strong dev infra; framed as part of the 'death of midwit software engineering.'
notes: X article — long-form. Highly relevant to how AI is changing engineering process.
content: Dan Robinson (@danlovesproofs) — "The Death of Issue Tracking". Notices forward-thinking engineering teams running with no Linear, no backlog, no issue tracking at all, scaling this to 30+ engineers. Post-backlog thinking: if it matters it'll come up again; why write a ticket when you can just fix it (cost to fix is converging on cost to understand); a blank page forces a real OODA loop and better prioritization. Tailwind is AI — 7 capable engineers running agent swarms deliver what took 50+. Requires small flat teams, high ambient context, engineers trusted to make independent judgment calls and do their own PM, and agent-ready dev infra. Cites Tuomas Artman (Linear) and Quinn Slack (Amp 'Fix Bugs Now'). Part of the broader 'death of Midwit Software Engineering.'

author: Avid
subject: Post by Avid on X
summary: Engagement-style 'AI will take your job' listicle, but with a usable career playbook: audit which tasks AI can replicate, learn local agent tooling and stackable 'skills', layer AI-native competence (prompting, orchestration, RAG, automation), shift from executor to system designer, double down on human edge, and build a product/brand.
notes: Engagement farming format but touches on real AI displacement concerns
content: Avid (@Av1dlive) — "AI layoffs are here (and how to save yourself)". Engagement-farming framing but a real career playbook: (1) Job Moat Audit — score each weekly task on AI-replicability; safe roles need judgment, relationships, taste, in-person accountability. (2) Learn local agent tooling + stack modular 'Skills' (Gmail/Calendar/GitHub/research/coding); leverage shifts to the orchestrator of agents; warns community skills carry security risks — review what you install. (3) Layer AI-native competence: prompting, agent orchestration, RAG on private data, workflow automation, multimodal, light fine-tuning. (4) Shift executor → system designer. (5) Double down on human edge. (6) Build a product/personal brand.

author: David Byttow
subject: Post by David Byttow on X
summary: Essay arguing AI agents are collapsing the coordination layer of organizations. As execution and coordination costs approach zero, the remaining scarce skill is goal clarity, taste, and ownership. Warns bad judgment now scales faster — strategic mistakes show up as fast delivery of the wrong thing.
notes: X article — strong think piece on AI eliminating coordination overhead in engineering orgs
content: David Byttow (@davidbyttow) — "AI absorbed the process layer". Agents are collapsing the coordination layer (task decomposition, sprint planning, dependency graphs, status syncs). Writing code was never the bottleneck — coordination overhead was, and that's commoditizing. What remains is judgment, taste, direction, ownership, and the ability to define a goal precisely enough that autonomous systems can act on it. AI won't replace managers; it exposes the ones whose contribution was procedural rather than directional. Leverage inverts: one sharp operator orchestrating agent fleets covers what took 5–10 people. Darker side: bad judgment scales faster — strip away the natural checkpoints of the coordination layer and strategic mistakes announce themselves through fast delivery of the wrong thing. Selection pressure on leadership judgment is about to intensify.

author: tetsuo
subject: Post by tetsuo on X
summary: Technical breakdown of recurring agentic failure modes in fast/distilled code models: wrong direct-exec vs shell selection, structured-output (JSON-only) non-compliance, and tool-result grounding failures (reporting success after a tool error). Fix: distill full agent trajectories (request→tool→output→grounded response), add contrastive near-miss examples, and gate on concrete agentic evals.
notes: X article — deep technical analysis of where distilled models fail at agentic tasks
content: tetsuo (@tetsuoai) — "Agentic Fine-Tuning Gaps in Fast and Distilled Models". Three recurring failure modes that base models get right but distilled/fast models miss: (1) Action selection & API semantics — direct execution (command+args) vs shell invocation; needs contrastive near-miss examples (wrong call, correct call, why). (2) Instruction priority & output discipline — 'return JSON only' compliance; training teaches the habit, constrained decoding enforces the contract. (3) State tracking & belief updating — the worst one: model gets an explicit tool error then reports success anyway; it's pattern-completing, not grounding. Fix: distill full agent trajectories (user request → tool choice → tool output → grounded response), not just final answers. Eval gates: argv correctness, JSON validity/schema exactness, contradiction rate. Includes a detailed eval prompt covering all three modes, and a note that desktop tools in Docker can't reach the host's localhost.

author: Sandhya
subject: Post by Sandhya on X
summary: 'BREAKING'-style but substantive thread on LMCache, an open-source KV-cache layer (6.9K stars, used by Google Cloud, CoreWeave, NVIDIA) that makes the LLM KV cache persistent and shareable across engine instances, tiered GPU→DRAM→disk→S3. Enables instant RAG, disaggregated prefill, and context sharing; integrates with vLLM and SGLang. Apache-2.0, pip install lmcache.
notes: Engagement farming format (BREAKING) but LMCache is a legit open-source project with real enterprise adoption
content: Sandhya (@agenticgirl) — engagement-style ('BREAKING') but real: LMCache, an open-source KV-cache layer (6.9K stars, 124K downloads/mo; used by Google Cloud, CoreWeave, NVIDIA). Problem: LLM inference recomputes the entire context (system prompt, history, retrieved docs) every request, burning ~50% of compute. LMCache makes the KV cache persistent and shareable across engine instances, offloading tiered GPU→CPU DRAM→local disk→S3. Unlocks: (1) Instant RAG — process a 100-page PDF once, near-zero TTFT on later queries; (2) Disaggregated prefill — prefill on H100s, stream cache to cheaper L4s for decode; (3) Context sharing across users. Claims 3–10x latency savings in multi-round QA. Integrates with vLLM and SGLang. Apache-2.0, pip install lmcache. GitHub repo in comments.

author: Joseph Thacker
subject: Post by Joseph Thacker on X
summary: Argues bug bounty / security research changed step-function fast in late 2025: coding agents went from not working to genuinely working. Example: pointed Claude Code at a target's scope (enumerate subdomains, analyze JS bundles, fuzz, test IDORs/GraphQL, write an HTML report); it ran ~30 min, self-recovered from auth/WAF errors, and returned two confirmed vulns. Hunters now build and share custom Claude Code skill libraries (JS static analysis, authenticated fuzzing, IDOR, GraphQL introspection). Quote-tweets Karpathy's parallel observation about programming.
notes: Concrete example of Claude Code being used for autonomous security research — impressive real-world agent use case
content:
Joseph Thacker (@rez0__): Bug bounty changed step-function fast — coding agents basically didn't work for security research before December and basically work now (higher quality, long-term coherence, tenacity). Example: pointed Claude Code at a new program's scope ('enumerate subdomains, grab JS bundles, run the full analysis pipeline — endpoints/secrets/source-sink/postMessage, fuzz paths, spider authenticated surface, check IDORs, test GraphQL, write an HTML report'). It ran ~30 min, hit auth failures/WAF blocks/malformed responses, researched and resolved each, and returned two confirmed vulns plus leads — untouched. Workflow is now spinning up agents, giving targets in English, managing output in parallel. The prize is ascending abstraction layers to long-running orchestrator agents with the right skills/memory/instructions. He and friends build and share custom Claude Code skill libraries (JS static analysis, authenticated fuzzing, IDOR frameworks, GraphQL introspection); each person's agent improves as the collective skill set grows. Still needs high-level direction, judgment, hacker intuition.

It is hard to communicate how much programming has changed due to AI in the last 2 months... coding agents basically didn't work before December.

author: Thariq
subject: Post by Thariq on X
summary: Anthropic's Thariq on building Claude Code: designing an agent's action space is an art. Walks through the AskUserQuestion tool's evolution (ExitPlanTool param → output-format parsing → dedicated tool), the shift from TodoWrite to the Task tool as models improved, replacing RAG with a Grep search tool so Claude builds its own context, progressive disclosure via Skills, and the Claude Code Guide subagent. Lesson: revisit tool assumptions as capabilities grow; experiment, read outputs, 'see like an agent.'
notes: From Anthropic engineer — 3.7M views. Essential reading on agent tool design philosophy behind Claude Code.
content: Thariq (@trq212) — "Lessons from Building Claude Code: Seeing like an Agent". Constructing an agent's action space is one of the hardest parts of building a harness. Tools should be shaped to the model's abilities; you learn those by reading its outputs and experimenting. Examples: (1) Elicitation/AskUserQuestion — tried adding questions to ExitPlanTool (confused plan vs questions), then a markdown output format (unreliable), finally a dedicated tool that blocks the loop and shows a modal; Claude 'liked' calling it. (2) Tasks & Todos — launched with TodoWrite + every-5-turn reminders; as models improved the reminders became constraining, so replaced with the Task tool (dependencies, cross-subagent coordination). (3) Search — replaced a fragile RAG vector DB with a Grep tool so Claude builds its own context; formalized progressive disclosure via Agent Skills (recursive file reads). (4) Claude Code Guide subagent — progressive disclosure to answer 'how do I use Claude Code' without bloating the system prompt. Claude Code has ~20 tools; the bar to add one is high. Designing tools is an art, not a science — experiment often, read your outputs, see like an agent.

author: Sudo su
subject: Post by Sudo su on X
summary: Practical steering tips for local coding agents: tell the model its own architecture/constraints (e.g. Qwen3.5 fires 8 of 256 experts/token), give project structure over single prompts, iterate in layers (scaffold → refine → polish), let it debug its own failures, and use long context (262K) to hold the whole project. Notes Claude Code as a solid harness and that llama.cpp merged native Anthropic endpoints (no proxy/LiteLLM). Argues the skill gap in local inference is now the harness and steering, not the models — all on a single RTX 3090.
content:
How to steer local coding agents: (1) tell the model what it is — paste its architecture/constraints (Qwen3.5 has 256 experts, 8 fire per token); (2) project structure over single prompts — give it a directory, files, a goal; (3) iterate in layers: scaffold, refine, polish; (4) let it fail and debug itself — that's the agent loop working; (5) use long context (262K) to hold the whole project. Claude Code is a solid harness (17K-token system prompt loads clean, no tool-use errors); llama.cpp merged native Anthropic endpoints so there's no proxy/LiteLLM layer. The skill gap in local inference is the harness and steering, not the models — all on a single RTX 3090.

Gave Qwen3.5-35B-A3B one prompt: build a cloud GPU marketplace with pricing cards, deploy templates, and a benchmark leaderboard. It planned the layout, wrote animations, populated data, and served it — one shot, one HTML file, on a single consumer GPU.

author: SightBringer
subject: Post by SightBringer on X
summary: Critique of AI auto-memory (quote-tweeting Anthropic's Claude auto-memory announcement) as a "power grab disguised as convenience": capturing your patterns, defaults, and definitions of 'good' shifts the model from serving you to shaping you, and dependency is the business model. Counter-playbook: treat memory as a controlled instrument — scope it like permissions, separate persona from operations, keep a purge cycle, audit what the model believes about you, and never let it silently rewrite your intent. Frames the AI era as shifting from intelligence to custody of context/continuity.
notes: Interesting counterpoint on AI memory features — worth considering for our own tool adoption strategy
content:
Auto memory is a power grab disguised as a convenience feature — it slowly owns your workflow, then your judgment, then your dependency graph. The real prize is your patterns, defaults, priorities, blind spots, and private definitions of 'good'; once captured, the model stops serving you and starts shaping you, and that dependency spiral is the business model. Done right, memory is a weapon: scope it like permissions, separate persona from operations, keep a purge cycle, audit what the model believes about you, and never let it silently rewrite your intent. The AI era stops being about intelligence and becomes about custody — who holds the continuity and context. If you don't control that, you're renting your own mind back from a platform.

We've rolled out a new auto-memory feature. Claude now remembers what it learns across sessions — project context, debugging patterns, preferred approaches — and recalls it later without you writing anything down.

author: George from prodmgmt.world
subject: Post by George from 🕹prodmgmt.world on X
summary: George argues elite PMs escalate documentation with validation rather than starting with a 10-page PRD: 1-pagers are decision docs (write ~10, ~3 get approved), 3-pagers validate and align, and 5-pagers add execution detail only for ideas that survived two rounds. Documentation should match your confidence level, not your anxiety level.
notes: Not AI-specific but solid product management process
content:
George from prodmgmt.world @nurijanian
Most PMs write documentation backwards, starting with 10-page PRDs nobody reads.
Elite PMs use three document types, each escalating with validation:
1-pagers = decision documents (problem, one success metric, three approaches, cost estimate, open questions). No user stories/wireframes. Goal: stakeholder yes/no. Write ~10, maybe 3 get approved.
3-pagers = validate and align (user research quotes, metrics w/ baselines, high-level solution, risks/mitigation, dependencies). Engineers read in 10 min.
5-pagers = execution detail only for validated ideas (user stories w/ acceptance criteria, wireframes/prototypes, system design, instrumentation, launch checklist).
Key: don't jump straight to 5-pager. Documentation should match confidence level, not anxiety level.

author: Avi Chawla
subject: Post by Avi Chawla on X
summary: Avi Chawla revives Norm Hardy's 1988 'confused deputy problem' as the defining security issue for autonomous agents that hold real credentials but can't judge intent. He points to Teleport's open-source Agentic Identity Framework, which gives each agent a unique cryptographic identity, evaluates access in real time, auto-discovers shadow agents and unmanaged MCP servers, and keeps full audit trails.
notes: Good framing of AI agent security through a classic CS security lens
content:
Avi Chawla @_avichawla
A 37-year-old paper is trending in AI: Norm Hardy's 1988 "confused deputy problem" - a program with legitimate access gets tricked into misusing it on behalf of someone who shouldn't have it. The program has authority to act but can't tell whether a request is legitimate.
Now relevant because we give AI agents access to production DBs, internal APIs, email, cloud infra and let them operate autonomously. Agents are powerful enough to cause damage but anonymous enough that you can't trace/contain them. Better credentials don't fix it.
Teleport's open-source Agentic Identity Framework treats identity as the control plane: each agent gets a unique cryptographic identity (actions trace to a specific actor), access evaluated in real-time and scoped to need, shadow agents/unmanaged MCP servers auto-discovered, anomalous behavior visible with full audit trails. GitHub link in replies.

author: Atlas Forge
subject: Post by Atlas Forge on X
summary: Long-form piece on nine 'meta-learning loops' that let an agent improve across sessions, not just within one: failure-to-guardrail pipelines, tiered memory with trust scoring and decay, prediction-outcome calibration, nightly extraction, friction detection, expiring context holds, plus cognitive loops (epistemic tagging, creative-mode directives, recursive self-improvement). Start with a regressions list; the key is closing the loops so learning compounds.
notes: X article — directly applicable to how we structure CLAUDE.md and agent memory
content:
Atlas Forge @AtlasForgeAI (X article, automated by @jonnym1ller)
"How to Build Nine Meta-Learning Loops for Your OpenClaw Agent". TL;DR: agents are smart within sessions, stupid across them; fix is structural feedback loops in the agent's files so failures become guardrails, predictions become calibration, friction becomes signal. Start with a regressions list.
Three levels: reactive (fix what broke, add a rule), reflective (extract patterns), generative (system improves the system).
The nine loops: 1) Failure-to-Guardrail pipeline (each failure -> one-line rule in boot file). 2) Tiered memory with trust scoring + decay (constitutional/strategic/operational tiers, trust 0-1, hit counts, supersede chains). 3) Prediction-outcome calibration (write predictions, log delta+lesson to surface systematic bias). 4) Nightly extraction (11pm cron reviews the day, "context is cache not state" test). 5) Friction detection (log contradictions instead of silent compliance to avoid architectural drift). 6) Active context holds (expiring filters, inspired by vasocomputation). 7-9 cognitive: epistemic tagging ([consensus]/[observed]/[inferred]/[speculative]/[contrarian]), creative-mode directives, recursive self-improvement (generate/evaluate/diagnose/improve, stop after 3 iters <5% gain).
Three mistakes that kill learning: confusing RAG with learning, optimizing within sessions not across, building loops that never close. "The question isn't how smart is your agent, it's how fast is it learning."

author: Aakash Gupta
subject: Post by Aakash Gupta on X
summary: Aakash Gupta, building on a Karpathy quote, argues agents are the new distribution channel for software: they call CLIs and MCP servers and read docs programmatically rather than browsing marketing sites. MCP hit 97M monthly SDK downloads and 10k+ servers in a year and was donated to the Linux Foundation. Winners of the next 24 months build agent-accessible surface area (CLIs, MCP endpoints, machine-readable docs) now.
notes: X article — strong case for MCP as the new API surface. 85% of enterprises expected to have AI agents deployed.
content:
Aakash Gupta @aakashgupta (quoting Andrej Karpathy)
Karpathy's point: the new distribution channel for software is agents. Agents don't browse marketing sites, watch demos, or click onboarding - they call your CLI, hit your MCP server, read docs programmatically. If those surfaces don't exist, your product is invisible to them.
MCP went 0 to 97M monthly SDK downloads in 12 months, 10,000+ active servers, adopted by OpenAI, Google DeepMind, Microsoft, Cloudflare; Anthropic donated MCP to the Linux Foundation by Dec 2025. Running an MCP server is the new baseline for product discovery. 85% of enterprises expected to deploy AI agents needing CLIs, MCP endpoints, machine-readable docs.
Karpathy quote: CLIs are exciting precisely because they're "legacy" tech - battle-tested, standardized, universally parseable (stdin/stdout, flags, JSON); the Unix philosophy was accidentally designed for AI agents. Winners of next 24 months build agent-accessible surface area now; losers optimize their landing page above the fold.

author: Rohit
subject: Post by Rohit on X
summary: Rohit's 10-step checklist for taking agentic AI from demo to production (40%+ of projects fail on architecture, not models): threat-model boundaries, strict input/output contracts, RBAC + sandboxing, layered/compact context, governed grounding, planning/orchestration as control flow, memory in the architecture, native retry/error handling, full observability, and governance with safety gates and drift detection.
notes: Good production readiness checklist for agent systems
content:
Rohit @rohit4verse
Over 40% of agentic AI projects fail not because of models but poor architecture. 10-step checklist to reach production:
1. boundaries: define threat models early. 2. contracts: strict input/output schemas. 3. security: role-based access + strict sandboxing. 4. context: keep context windows layered and compact. 5. knowledge: treat grounding as a governed tool. 6. control: planning/orchestration as control flow. 7. state: build memory into the architecture. 8. reliability: native retry and error-handling loops. 9. observability: traces, metrics, logs. 10. governance: safety gates and drift detection. Deep dive in linked article "how to build a production grade ai agent."

Matt Pocock @mattpocockuk
Under-emphasized: AI coding demands a 'lead dev' mentality. If you spent your pre-AI career leveling up teammates (through API design, feedback loops, architecture), working with AI feels natural. If you only focused on your own output, it feels super bad.

author: Dr Milan Milanović
subject: Post by Dr Milan Milanović on X
summary: Milan Milanović makes the case for git worktree (shipped in Git 2.5, July 2015): check out multiple branches into separate directories that share one .git, avoiding stashing and duplicate clones. The standout modern use case is AI agents - give each of 3-5 parallel Claude Code/Cursor/Codex agents its own isolated worktree and branch so they don't overwrite each other.
notes: Not AI-specific but relevant — Claude Code uses worktrees for agent isolation
content:
Dr Milan Milanović @milan_milanovic
"Git Worktree: The Feature You've Had Since 2015 But Never Used." git worktree lets you check out multiple branches into separate directories simultaneously - no stashing, WIP commits, or double-cloning. A worktree is a separate working directory with its own branch/index/HEAD, but all worktrees share one .git directory so history/objects exist once. Commands: git worktree add ../hotfix -b hotfix/critical-bug main; git worktree list; git worktree remove ../hotfix. Beats stash and multiple clones (lightweight, changes immediately visible).
AI agent use case: running Claude Code, Cursor, or Codex, multiple agents on one working dir overwrite each other. Worktrees give each agent its own isolated dir and branch - run 3-5 agents in parallel. Cursor uses worktrees under the hood; Codex runs on dedicated background worktrees. Constraint: can't check out same branch in two worktrees (workaround: temp local branch). Shipped July 2015.

author: Aman
subject: Post by Aman on X
summary: Aman shares Garry Tan's (CEO of Y Combinator) CLAUDE.md prompt for Claude Code, which he uses to ship 4,000+ line features with full tests in about an hour. The prompt makes Claude act like a senior engineer: judge whether a plan is over/under-engineered before coding, do a structured review (architecture -> code quality -> tests -> performance), present tradeoffs with opinionated recommendations, and pause for feedback before implementing.
notes: YC CEO's CLAUDE.md — directly relevant to our own setup
content: CEO of Y Combinator shared his CLAUDE.md prompt for Claude Code; it helps him ship 4,000+ line features with full tests in about an hour. The prompt pushes Claude to decide if the plan is overbuilt/underbuilt/'engineered enough' before writing code, aggressively review test coverage/edge cases/failure modes, look for performance and scaling risks. The workflow: structured review (architecture->code quality->tests->performance), present tradeoffs with opinionated recommendations, pause for feedback. author: Dr Milan Milanovic
subject: Post by Dr Milan Milanović on X
summary: Milan Milanovic's thesis: AI won't replace developers so much as the software-development process we're used to. Code is becoming cheap while decisions become expensive; AI reduces typing, not thinking. Developers who only implement tasks will struggle, while those who understand the product, domain, and system architecture will thrive.
notes: Good framing for leadership discussions about AI adoption
content: The fear isn't that AI will replace developers, it's that AI will replace the software development process we're used to. Code is becoming cheap; decisions are becoming expensive. AI can write functions all day but can't decide what should be built or why. AI reduces typing, not thinking; it exposes shallow understanding and rewards clarity, reasoning, ownership. Developers who only implement tasks will struggle; those who understand product, domain, and system will thrive. Small teams will ship things that once required enterprises.

author: abhi
subject: Post by abhi on X
summary: A detailed playbook for building continually-improving AI agents: define the business metric first, think in terms of many per-message/session/long-tail signals (not just thumbs up/down), design UI that makes signal collection easy, build signal-derived few-shot rankers, and A/B test every change against a control group. Includes a long copy-paste prompt for having Claude Code wire feedback loops into an existing agent. Warns about reward hacking when over-optimizing a single signal.
notes: X article — practical agent improvement framework from someone with production agent experience
content: Why your AI agents still don't work. Most agents are horrible at integrating with domain-specific knowledge and adapting to feedback. RAG/memory/engineering tricks aren't enough; feedback loops are inevitable for every agent builder. PART 1 Prepare your system: Step 0 define the agent's end goal / business metric (retention, conversions, usage) and a core thesis (engagement time vs tool usage vs fastest goal completion). Step 1 think in signals - hundreds of per-message, session-level, and week/month signals (time-to-user-response, abandonment, sentiment, follow-up clicked). Beware too few signals -> reward hacking / over-optimization. Step 2 design a UI that makes signal collection easy (Cursor tab feature, follow-up selectors, link tracking). PART 2 Build feedback loops: Step 3 simple signal-derived rankers feeding few-shot examples (~10 max) weighted by signal quality; highest-lift improvement. Step 4 run A/B experiments with control vs experiment group and business-metric tracking (~50k msgs/group for B2C; posthog/mixpanel). Step 5 a long copy-paste prompt to have Claude Code analyze your codebase and implement the full feedback-loop system (signal collection, storage, embedding-based ranker with final_score = semantic_similarity * signal_quality_score, A/B framework, monitoring). TLDR: define business outcomes, think in signals, build a ranking system, experiment. Agentic systems are precisely engineered, not vibe-coded.

author: Sumanth
subject: Post by Sumanth on X
summary: Overview of PageIndex, an open-source 'vectorless' RAG framework that drops vector databases and arbitrary chunking. It builds an LLM-optimized hierarchical tree (like a table of contents) and uses reasoning-based tree search to navigate documents the way a human expert would, giving traceable page-level references. Powers Mafin 2.5, which hits 98.7% on FinanceBench. GitHub link in comments.
notes: More detailed technical breakdown of PageIndex than the Sukh Sroay post
content: Document Index for Vectorless, Reasoning-based RAG! PageIndex is an open-source RAG framework that removes vector databases and chunking from document retrieval. Most RAG systems rely on semantic similarity - chunk arbitrarily, embed, retrieve by what looks similar. But similarity != relevance. Professional docs (financial reports, legal filings, technical manuals) need multi-step reasoning and domain expertise; vector search falls short when every section shares terminology. PageIndex builds a hierarchical tree structure (like a table of contents optimized for LLMs) then uses reasoning-based tree search. The LLM reasons about document structure ('debt trends are usually in the financial summary or Appendix G'). Features: no vector DB/embedding pipelines, no artificial chunking, traceable page-level references, reasoning-based navigation. Powers Mafin 2.5 at 98.7% accuracy on FinanceBench. 100% open source; GitHub link in comments.

author: Mischa van den Burg
subject: Post by Mischa van den Burg on X
summary: Argues AI agent orchestration is the next 'container orchestration war', building on a Steve Yegge framework of two primitives: Workers (making a single agent produce high-quality output) vs Factories (coordinating thousands of work items across many agents). Maps these to familiar infra patterns - Workers = CI runners/pods/Lambdas, Factories = schedulers/control planes/workflow engines. Predicts 'nondeterministic idempotence' as the new eventual consistency, audit trails for AI work, GitOps-style declarative state, and reuse of the microservices observability stack. Kubernetes-fluent engineers have a head start.
notes: Steve Yegge framework — maps agent orchestration to k8s/DevOps patterns we already know
content:
Kubernetes won the container orchestration war. But we're about to fight a new one: AI agent orchestration. Steve Yegge dropped a framework every DevOps engineer needs. Two fundamental primitives: Factories vs Workers. Workers = how to make a single agent produce high-quality output (= CI runners, pods, Lambda). Factories = how to coordinate thousands of work items across many agents (= schedulers, control planes, workflow engines). Takeaways: agent orchestration follows container-orchestration patterns; 'Nondeterministic Idempotence' is the new eventual consistency; audit trails for AI work become as critical as infra audit logs; 'work itself as primitive' mirrors GitOps declarative state; expect the same observability stack as microservices. Teams that know Kubernetes deeply (control loops, reconciliation, declarative state) have a massive head start.

Steve Yegge: birthday update post; things moving so fast he posts a gigantic update every week to catch people up.
